energy.notes: About 35 on-site natural-gas turbines (a combined 422 MW per SELC and aerial imagery) supplement a 150 MW grid substation and Tesla Megapack storage. The Shelby County Health Department permitted only 15 turbines; an SELC/NAACP appeal stays that permit. xAI said the Phase-I temporary turbines would be demobilized after grid connection, but 2025 aerial imagery shows 35 on site.

water.notes: The open cooling loop's design draw is roughly 3 MGD from the Memphis Sands aquifer via MLGW (xAI engineer, per Governing), though Protect Our Aquifer reported a lower early-operational actual of ~0.8 MGD (~812,000 gal/day) in early 2025 as the site was still ramping. xAI broke ground in Oct 2025 on an $80M greywater-recycling plant at the T.E. Maxson wastewater facility (targeting ~10 MGD of treated wastewater); construction was later paused.

community.notes: The NAACP (with SELC and Earthjustice) sued xAI over roughly 35 unpermitted methane gas turbines; the county permitted only 15, and the NAACP/SELC appeal to the Shelby County Air Pollution Control Board stays it. The permit drew 1,700+ public comments, most opposed, and South Memphis (Boxtown) residents already face elevated asthma and cancer rates. xAI did not pursue a Shelby County EDGE PILOT tax abatement; no public subsidy is on record.

energy.notes: 100% renewable match via PPAs and RECs; Meta contracted 437 MW of new Oregon solar, including about 100 MW in the Prineville area. 2023 average PUE 1.08; no on-site generation.

water.notes: Outdoor-air economizer cooling with evaporative assist on peak days. Annual withdrawal was ~68.4M gallons (2022), down from a ~117.5M-gallon peak (2020); use concentrates on roughly 10 hot summer days (about 10% of city consumption on those days). Meta co-funded a second aquifer storage-and-recovery injection well.

subsidies.program: Oregon Long-Term Rural Enterprise Zone — 15-year property-tax abatement across multiple building phases since 2012 (per-company cumulative total not publicly disclosed)

community.notes: Broadly supportive — credited with reversing post-recession unemployment; city franchise fees from data-center power grew from ~$430K (2011) to ~$11M/year (2024). But Crook County schools forgo tens of millions per year in abated taxes, biologists have flagged aquifer-drawdown concerns, and in 2026 Oregon moved to pause data-center enterprise-zone exemptions for new applicants. A June 2026 contractor change cut 66 logistics jobs (Meta said most would be rehired).

Concerns span water (Yakama Nation fishers and Dog River endangered-fish habitat), school funding, and abatement scale; Oregon enacted a 2025–26 pause on new data-center incentives.

Equinix is eligible for Virginia's statewide data-center sales-and-use tax exemption, but state law bars disclosure of individual recipients and amounts.

DISCREPANCY: the existing poweredBy field says "on-site natural gas generators" (the adjacent independently-owned Lordstown Energy Center is a separate gas plant); on-site generation for the data center is not confirmed in site-specific filings. The 1,500 MW figure is a long-term campus ceiling — the initial data center uses "a small part" of the footprint.

water.notes: SoftBank spokesperson: "The Lordstown site will also use a closed-loop cooling system that is designed to reduce water consumption." Oracle co-CEO Clay Magouyrk (via AP): "These data centers are designed to not use water." No site-specific MGD figure published.

notes: Planned 1,500 MW is a long-term campus ceiling, not a near-term operational figure: a SoftBank spokesperson said the initial SoftBank/OpenAI data center uses "a small part" of the 6.2M-sq-ft site, which also hosts a Foxconn/SoftBank hub manufacturing modular data-center equipment. Crescent Dune LLC (a SoftBank affiliate) acquired the site from Foxconn for $375M (Sept 2025, Trumbull County Auditor). SoftBank's "up to $3 billion" is a ceiling covering both manufacturing and the data center — no data-center-only investment figure is confirmed.

QTS declines to disclose the campus's power capacity (third-party trackers estimate over 650 MW at full build-out); it reports portfolio-wide carbon-free electricity for 2024.

subsidies.program: Eagle Mountain RDA Sweetwater #3 community-reinvestment tax-increment financing — reimburses QTS/tenants 61% of real-property and 74% of personal-property tax increment, 20 years per phase (up to 40 years total); first-phase increment projected at ~$98.86M

community.notes: City officials are formally supportive (a projected ~$12M/year municipal energy tax funds roads, schools, and first responders), and no organized opposition to QTS specifically was found. But water and energy transparency are actively debated in arid Utah — the 2026 state water-reporting law (HB76) was driven partly by Eagle Mountain's data-center growth, though facilities operational before July 2026 are exempt.

energy.notes: Grid-served via PPL Electric (PJM); developers committed ~$200M with PPL for grid upgrades including two on-site substations, and the community benefits agreement requires 100% clean energy. An initial 100 MW phase is planned with potential expansion toward 300 MW.

water.notes: Air-cooled, closed-loop design; the developer states no municipal water will be used in cooling. The community benefits agreement caps water at 20,000 gal/day per campus — a claimed >80% reduction from the former printing plants on the sites.

community.notes: Repurposing two former printing plants drew strong resident opposition (about 20 speakers against, none in favor, at one hearing) and a challenge to the zoning classification that bypassed public hearings; the city is drafting a data-center-specific ordinance. Notably, the developer is receiving no local tax breaks — the city confirmed LERTA and CRIZ do not apply — and instead signed a 20-year, ~$20M community benefits agreement, described as the first data-center-specific CBA in the US. Reported job figures conflict: the binding agreement cites ~2,000 construction and 350 permanent, while CoreWeave's own release cites ~600 construction and 70–175 permanent.

Bitcoin miner's $640m megadeal stumbles as Texans sour on industry's promise of jobs and growth

notes: Phase I (400 MW) operational for Bitcoin mining with immersion cooling. AMD contracted 50 MW for HPC workloads. Riot developing 112 MW Core and Shell infrastructure at Corsicana for future hyperscale AI tenants. Campus acquired in phases to 858 total acres. ERCOT-approved for 1 GW total. Navarro County's October 2024 abatement records cite a considered $640M investment and 140 new jobs; the signed agreement sets a $380M minimum taxable value and 140 FTE by January 1, 2026 (a Riot spokesperson separately cited 180 jobs).

water.notes: Immersion cooling for the operational 400 MW Bitcoin-mining phase; cooling for the future AI/HPC phase is not yet confirmed. The facility is projected to consume up to 1.5 million gallons of water per day at peak summer demand under a city-water agreement — a seasonal peak, not an average daily rate.

subsidies.program: Chapter 312 property-tax abatement, Reinvestment Zone 24-102 (10-year term from tax year 2025)

community.notes: Organized local opposition (Concerned Citizens of Navarro County; Change.org petition). County commissioners declined an earlier, larger reinvestment-zone request in March 2024 after resident pushback; a smaller Chapter 312 abatement passed on a 3-2 vote in October 2024. Cited concerns: noise, peak water use, ERCOT grid strain, and limited permanent jobs relative to power draw.

Phase II (260MW critical IT load) in development, deliveries expected H1 2027. CoreWeave's total commitment across all phases now 526MW critical IT load. Total approved campus capacity 1.63GW, potential to scale to 3.6GW. Campus now spans 2,200+ acres (expanded from the original 160-acre site).

water.notes: Phase 1 (133 MW) is a liquid-cooled AI deployment with 90+ chillers and associated piping (Clayco). Chiller condenser type (air-cooled vs. water-cooled/evaporative) and water-consumption (MGD) not published. Investment/jobs: Phase 1 financed by a $1.4B debt facility + $350M Galaxy equity; Phase 2 permits estimate $3.5B (Helios 2 ~$900M, Helios 3 & 4 ~$1.3B each). notes: Campus at intersection of Perkins Road and Richmond Road, north of Stillwater. Coordinates are approximate (city centroid shifted north). Up to 6 data center buildings, each 300,000 sq ft. Phase I targeted for 2027. City Council acceptance of final plat required before recording with Payne County Clerk. Job figures are projections (~30 permanent per phase; construction cited as 'thousands') - not recorded as confirmed counts. Oklahoma's Sec. 1357 data-center sales-tax exemption applies by statute but no Stillwater-specific award amount is published.

Cooling mechanism not specified in public sources.

subsidies.program: 25-year ad valorem (property-tax) exemption via a PILOT agreement with Stillwater/Payne County taxing jurisdictions (Stillwater Public Schools, Meridian Technology Center, Payne County, county health dept); PILOT payments rise 1%/yr, estimated ~$58.2M over 25 years for the first two phases

community.notes: All local taxing jurisdictions approved the 25-year incentive agreement, but Payne County Commissioner Rhonda Markum cast the lone dissenting vote, citing water and transparency concerns (officials were required to sign NDAs before learning project details). Per the Muskogee County 'Springboard' Project Plan (June 2025), whose applicant is "Acacia House Group LLC" — "a leading U.S.-based data center operator"; Google is NOT explicitly named in the plan, though it is the only operator to have publicly announced Muskogee County campuses.

name: Google Pryor Data Center Campus (Mayes County Expansion)

energy.notes: Google's second-largest data center campus in the world. Oklahoma wind generates ~42% of in-state electricity. New expansion announced as part of $9B Oklahoma investment (August 2025). Google has invested $5.7B+ in Oklahoma historically (prior to August 2025 $9B commitment).

statusHistory.note: Campus opens in Mayes County after first announcement in 2007. Has undergone three expansions since opening.

sources.label: Google planning another expansion for Pryor data center
Google to invest $9B in cloud and AI infrastructure in Oklahoma
As data centers boom in Oklahoma, so does water demand
Oklahoma lawmakers tried to cut a costly data-center incentive, but gave Google a break
Google announces support for local schools and nonprofits

notes: Campus operational since 2011; Google's second-largest data center globally per Alphabet CIO Ruth Porat. Four expansions now underway. $5.7B+ invested historically in Oklahoma prior to August 2025 $9B commitment. Expansion capacity and MW not publicly specified. Coordinates are Pryor city center.

water.notes: Utility records (open-records request) show the Pryor center used more than 1.1 billion gallons Jul 2024–Jun 2025 (≈3.0 MGD, a floor) from the Neosho River via the state-owned MidAmerica Industrial Park; ~253M gallons were discharged back to the river, the remainder lost to evaporative cooling. Google was the park's second-largest water user that year.

subsidies.program: Oklahoma ad valorem manufacturing exemption (5-year property-tax exemption on data-center equipment) — $45.9M in 2021 alone (largest single-year benefit; amountUsd reflects this 2021 figure); ~$239M reimbursed cumulatively over ~a decade for the Mayes County facility; existing qualifying equipment retains the exemption to 2036 under SB 609 (2021)

community.notes: Strong official support — Pryor Mayor Zac Doyle: Google has brought "hundreds of jobs, economic growth, and billions in investment" and supports local schools and nonprofits. Google reports more than 800 jobs created to date in Pryor and $4.4 billion invested in Mayes County to date (company-provided figures via The Frontier). Grassroots and state-level concerns are documented: local hiring practices, rising housing costs, and heavy water use (>1.1B gal/yr); the Oklahoma Water Resources Board warned (Oct 2025) that data centers "will contribute to the strain" on water supply. Subsidy scale also criticized: ~$239M ad valorem for this facility, plus $113.9M in income-tax credits Google has claimed statewide since 2017.

name: Stratos AI Data Center Campus
poweredBy: On-site natural gas (Ruby Pipeline) + planned solar array

energy.notes: Ruby Pipeline (680-mile interstate natural gas line) runs through Hansel Valley; 3,000-acre solar array planned; fully off-grid design

water.notes: Closed-loop system using on-site saline water described as unsuitable for human or agricultural use; developer states no diversion from Great Salt Lake basin

community.notes: Community opposition over fast-tracked MIDA process, CO2 emissions (~34M metric tons/year at full build), light/noise pollution, and Great Salt Lake impacts. Utah Senate President called for 75% footprint reduction; O'Leary agreed to cut acreage by 50% (remove ~20,000 acres). Governor Cox ordered 'careful consideration' of environmental impacts.

statusHistory.note: MIDA Board approved Stratos Project Area on April 24, 2026; Box Elder County Commission approved interlocal agreement May 4, 2026

sources.label: Stratos Project Fact Sheet — Box Elder County
Renderings show Stratos data center but permits could take years

notes: Hansel Valley and Locomotive Valley, western Box Elder County near Lucin, UT. 60 planned data centers on initial 10,000-acre phase. 9GW is full-buildout figure; Phase 1 ~3GW. Total cost projected $100B+ at full buildout; only $20M invested as of mid-2026. Designed by Gensler. Environmental permitting via Utah DEQ expected to take ~2 years. MIDA oversight provides state-level approval authority. Coordinates are approximate for western Box Elder County; exact site boundaries unpublished.

name: Joule Energy and Data Center Campus
poweredBy: On-site Caterpillar G3520K gas generators; nuclear planned post-2030

energy.notes: Phase 1: 1GW from Caterpillar gas generators with battery storage; potential scale to 4GW (some sources cite 12GW); nuclear exploration underway; adjacent Intermountain Power Project pipeline. Off-grid design.

water.notes: Closed-loop direct-to-chip cooling saves millions of gallons vs evaporative; family water rights held by founders; no new water allocation required

statusHistory.note: Millard County Commission approved project; site is conversion of family alfalfa farm
Groundbreaking ceremony held November 6, 2025 outside Holden, UT; first power delivery targeted Q4 2026

sources.label: Groundbreaking held for Joule data center campus in Millard County
Why this coming data center in Millard County is so unique

notes: Founders Mark McDougal and David Gray converting family 4,000-acre alfalfa farm near Holden. Described by founders as largest private project in Utah history. Partnership with Caterpillar for generator sets; MOU with EnergySolutions for nuclear feasibility study. Part of Governor Cox's Operation Gigawatt initiative. Site at 4,800+ ft elevation for cooling efficiency. Coordinates approximate for Holden, Millard County.

community.notes: The Millard County Commission approved the project (Aug 2025) and it is widely framed as a landmark rural-Utah investment (part of Gov. Cox's Operation Gigawatt). Local coverage also notes resident concern about the campus's scale, off-grid gas generation, and water; the founders' family holds the water rights. No organized opposition or litigation surfaced.

name: Creekstone Energy Delta Gigasite
poweredBy: On-site natural gas + utility-scale solar (SITLA lease) + nuclear planned

energy.notes: 43,000 Dth/day natural gas via executed interconnect; 13,000-acre SITLA solar lease (largest solar lease in Utah history, announced March 10, 2026); Torus battery/flywheel storage installed Dec 2025; MOU with EnergySolutions for 2GW+ nuclear feasibility (2030–2035 horizon). Off-grid design.

water.notes: Dry cooling design described as 'essentially waterless operation'

subsidies.program: Rural Economic Development Tax Increment Financing (REDTIF) — 50% post-performance tax credit
subsidies.jurisdiction: Utah Governor's Office of Economic Opportunity

statusHistory.note: Creekstone announced Delta Gigasite; committed $17B to Millard County project; Series B funding closed led by Trident Ridge
Broke ground December 2025 on Delta site; first 300MW gas-fired power delivery targeted H1 2027; permits issued

sources.label: Zeo Energy to supply 280MW for Creekstone's 10GW Delta Gigasite
Creekstone Energy Commits $17B to Millard County — Utah Governor's Office
sources.publisher: Utah Governor's Office of Economic Development

sources.label: Creekstone Energy Secures 13,000-Acre SITLA Solar Lease (Largest in Utah History)

notes: Claims to be world's largest planned AI data center campus at 20M sq ft. 1,143 acres owned; 13,000-acre SITLA lease for solar (largest solar lease in Utah history — record previously stated 30,000 acres, corrected to 13,000 acres per March 2026 BusinessWire announcement and multiple independent sources). Initial customer BluSky AI allocated up to 50MW. Adjacent to Intermountain Power Project. 10GW is full-buildout target; first 300MW gas delivery H1 2027. Coordinates at Delta city centroid; site is southeast of Delta.

name: Microsoft Malaga Campus

statusHistory.note: Microsoft acquired 102.5 acres on Malaga Alcoa Highway for $9.2M across three parcels
Permits filed for three data center buildings on Malaga Alcoa Highway; Chelan County
Construction of first datacenter building commenced June 2023 per Microsoft Local blog; Jumpoff Ridge Substation also under construction on campus

energy.notes: Carbon-free hydropower from Chelan PUD; 18 MW surplus hydropower through end of 2025, then long-term arrangement under utility energy marketing strategy. Under the June 2025 large-load contract, Microsoft will import new clean power from a third-party supplier for 2026-2040 (source undetermined); Chelan PUD is not obligated to serve its own hydropower. Microsoft funded the $86.5M Jumpoff Ridge substation.

water.notes: Newest AI datacenter buildings designed for no evaporative cooling water use per Microsoft Community-First blog. Earlier permit data shows ~83 gpm per building for fire suppression and employee use, not cooling.

sources.label: Microsoft Plans Three Data Centers in Malaga, Washington
Malaga Datacenter Project Overview
Microsoft Malaga Campus — 288 MW capacity profile
PUD commissioners approve power contract for Microsoft data center

notes: Three-building campus at 5375 Malaga Alcoa Highway; two additional buildings possible by 2027 on demand. 288 MW figure from secondary aggregator Cleanview only; primary sources (DCD, Microsoft) do not specify MW capacity. Construction of first building began June 2023 per Microsoft Local blog; original statusHistory incorrectly dated under_construction to 2024-05. Expected operational 2026. Coordinates approximate for Malaga area on Malaga Alcoa Highway. A Building-1 permit cites ~72 peak-shift staff (a single-building figure, not a facility employment total); no confirmed total campus capital-expenditure figure is public.

community.notes: Chelan County PUD commissioners approved Microsoft's large-load power contract on June 16, 2025 (the first under the PUD's new large-load framework), structured so Microsoft bears the full cost and risk of its demand. Public comments centered on preserving reliability and affordable rates for existing customers, with some concern about service priority during energy shortfalls. No litigation.

name: Meta Kuna Data Center

statusHistory.note: Project Peregrine: 485-acre site in Kuna, Ada County announced
Broke ground September 2022
Construction resumed after December 2022 pause; new AI-optimized design with denser GPU deployment support

energy.notes: 100% renewable energy committed; Meta adding 200 MW of renewable energy to Idaho grid as part of project

community.notes: Meta committed to build $70M water and sewer system donated to city of Kuna per Construction Equipment Guide (sourceIndex 1). Note: Idaho Business Review (sourceIndex 2) cites a '$100M water treatment facility' — figures may reflect different project phases or scopes. Idaho sales-tax exemption on construction materials and server equipment applies.

sources.label: Meta Resumes Construction of Idaho Data Center with New AI Design
Meta Hits Peak Construction on $800M Idaho Data Center
Meta's Mega Data Center in Kuna On Track (completion later revised to 2026)

notes: 960,000 sq ft campus (Meta's 15th US data center). Originally Project Peregrine. Construction paused December 2022, restarted October 2023 with AI-optimized architecture; new design 31% cheaper and half the build time. Peak construction employed 1,400 workers. Expected operational end of 2026. Water infrastructure cost sourced to CEG ($70M) but Idaho Business Review cites $100M — discrepancy unresolved; use CEG figure as primary. Coordinates are Kuna city centroid; exact site is west of city center.

name: Amazon Boardman Exascale Data Center Campus
poweredBy: Oregon grid (majority hydro); local retail utility PacifiCorp (Pacific Power)

statusHistory.note: Amazon purchased 1,300 acres from Threemile Canyon Farms dairy for $37M near Boardman. Planning documents indicate 20 buildings, 4 million sq ft, 1 GW capacity. Amazon stated development plans not final.

energy.notes: PacifiCorp (Pacific Power) is the local retail utility in Morrow County; AWS filed power complaints against PacifiCorp in November 2025 regarding insufficient capacity. BPA Columbia River hydroelectric generation underlies the Oregon grid and is a major component of what PacifiCorp delivers. AWS claims 95% renewable sourcing in Oregon. AWS also holds wind PPA (Leaning Juniper IIA, 98.4 MW) in same county.

sources.label: Amazon Purchases 1,300 Acres in Morrow County, Oregon
Oregon Communities Envision 9,100 Acres for New Data Centers
How Amazon data centers use water for cooling
Johnson Economics — Economic Impact Analysis of Prospective Exascale Data Center Development in Morrow County (Jan 2025)

notes: Amazon confirmed the land purchase ($37M per Morrow County property records, confirmed by multiple news sources) but stated development plans are not final. Planning documents submitted to Morrow County describe a 1 GW exascale campus with 20 buildings across 4 million sq ft; Johnson Economics estimated $8B-$12B total investment. Land previously owned by Threemile Canyon Farms dairy. Distinct from Amazon's existing ~800 acres of operating/under-construction OR facilities. Lincoln Chronicle source URL returned 403 on fetch — primary claim supported by DCD source. energy.utility corrected from 'Bonneville Power Administration' (wholesale) to 'PacifiCorp (Pacific Power)' (retail utility). Coordinates are Boardman city centroid; exact site not publicly confirmed. Johnson Economics' economic-impact analysis (Morrow County Planning Commission, Jan 2025) models the full 1 GW campus at roughly $8 billion investment (potential range $7-12 billion), about 6,400 construction FTE over an eight-year build (~800/year) and about 560 operations FTE, assuming an Oregon Strategic Investment Program (SIP) property-tax abatement — modeled projections for a still-proposed campus, not firm commitments.

water.notes: Reflects Amazon's documented Oregon cooling practice — primarily free-air cooling with evaporative assist, with up to ~96% of cooling water returned to local farmers. Planning documents model roughly 35 million gallons/year for the full 1 GW campus (a projection). The specific cooling design for this proposed campus is not confirmed.

name: SDC Umatilla Campus

statusHistory.note: Sabey Data Centers announced 60-acre land purchase in Umatilla; groundbreaking planned for 2024 (revised from Q1 2025 per DCD)

energy.notes: Oregon grid is 40% renewable with 100% target by 2040. Sabey committed to net-zero carbon-free energy by 2029, 11 years ahead of Oregon's state target. JetCool Technologies liquid cooling pilot testing underway.

sources.label: Sabey Data Centers Purchases 60 Acres in Umatilla, Oregon for 100MW+ Facility
Sabey Data Centers to Build New Data Center Campus in Umatilla, Oregon
Sabey Data Centers comes to Umatilla
Sabey Data Centers picks Oregon

notes: Two-building, 714,540 sq ft multi-tenant campus. Groundbreaking originally Q1 2025, revised to 2024 per DCD; construction start as of July 2026 not independently confirmed. Investment figures conflicting across sources ($520M in one report, ~$950M in another); investmentUsd omitted. Coordinates are Umatilla city centroid.

water.notes: Sabey states it is "relying less on water-based cooling" for this development; a JetCool Technologies microjet liquid-cooling pilot is underway. No MGD disclosed.

subsidies.program: Greater Umatilla Enterprise Zone — 15-year property-tax abatement (county commissioners approved Aug 15, 2023). Terms: >=$200M investment and >=10 FTE at >=130% of county average wage; a $2M community-investment fee (hospital district + river trail) plus $85K/yr school and $58K/yr fire-district fees. Total abatement value not disclosed.

community.notes: County commissioners approved the enterprise-zone agreement (Aug 15, 2023); the Port of Umatilla welcomed the project; the community-investment fee funds a hospital-district clinic and a river trail. No opposition recorded. Investment estimates conflict ($520M per DCD, $950M per Commercial Search) — estimates, not confirmed.

name: Meta Cheyenne Data Center
poweredBy: Black Hills Energy grid with planned renewable additions

statusHistory.note: Project Cosmo approved by Cheyenne City Council; Meta used shell company Goat Systems LLC during planning
Construction began months before official July 2024 Meta announcement; Fortis Construction serving as GC; 715,000 sq ft, at least two buildings

energy.notes: Served under Black Hills Energy Large Power Contract Service tariff with customized renewable procurement. Meta and Black Hills Energy partnered to add new renewable resources to the Wyoming grid. Note: Meta signed a separate January 2026 portfolio-wide PPA with TerraPower for up to 8 remote Natrium reactors (power delivery via grid, not at the Cheyenne site); no on-site nuclear generation planned at this location per cited sources.

community.notes: Meta announced Data Center Community Action Grants for Cheyenne schools and nonprofits in 2026. Wyoming tax incentives for data center operators cited as a deciding factor in site selection.

sources.label: Meta Officially Announces Data Center in Cheyenne, Wyoming
Meta Announces New Data Center, a Major Investment, in Cheyenne, Wyoming

notes: 715,000 sq ft campus at High Plains Business Park, Meta's 25th global data center. Originally Project Cosmo. Optimized for AI workloads with new H-departed design. Expected operational 2027. Coordinates approximate for High Plains Business Park in SE Cheyenne. Energy.notes corrected: removed on-site nuclear claim (Meta-TerraPower PPA is portfolio-wide grid delivery, not an on-site Cheyenne plant) and removed unverified $1.2B solar claim. jobs.sourceIndex corrected from 0 to 1 (Cheyenne LEADS is the source for the 1,000 construction jobs figure).

name: Microsoft Cheyenne Mega-Campus Expansion
poweredBy: Black Hills Energy (LPCS tariff); hydrogen fuel cell backup pilot completed with Caterpillar

statusHistory.note: Microsoft announced intent to purchase 3,200 acres: 200 acres at Bison Business Park on Wapiti Trail + 3,000 acres in Southeast Cheyenne, tripling Wyoming footprint

energy.notes: Large Power Contract Service (LPCS) tariff; Microsoft funds all grid infrastructure upgrades without raising residential rates. Hydrogen fuel cell pilot (Caterpillar) tested 48-hour backup power. Commitment to carbon-free energy matching fossil fuel consumption.

community.notes: Microsoft committed $68M to off-site infrastructure (roads, storm sewers, trails, water). Will not accept local property tax incentives. Contributed $11M to Laramie County tax base in 2025. Operating in Cheyenne since 2012.

sources.label: Microsoft Announces Intent to Expand Datacenter Operations in Cheyenne
Microsoft to Triple Its Cheyenne Data Center Footprint With 3,200-Acre Land Buy

notes: Described by Microsoft as a 'multi-gigawatt multi-decade ecosystem.' The 400 MW initial-target figure that appeared in the prior record was NOT found in either cited source and has been removed pending a citable source (neither the Microsoft newsroom announcement nor Cowboy State Daily mentions a specific MW figure). Separate from existing Cheyenne campus (since 2012) and the HR Ranch Road construction underway 2025-2026. Coordinates approximate for Bison Business Park / SE Cheyenne area.

name: Big Sky Digital Infrastructure Campus
poweredBy: NorthWestern Energy grid + on-site solar, wind, and battery storage

statusHistory.note: Big Sky Digital Infrastructure (BSDI) announced by EnCap-backed Quantica Infrastructure; 5,000-acre Yellowstone County campus north of Billings
Quantica filed interconnection applications with NorthWestern Energy for 1,100 MW at Big Sky Campus

energy.notes: Phase 1: 500 MW renewable + battery storage by 2030. Phase 2: additional 500 MW by 2031. 40,000 acres under lease for solar, wind, battery development. Grid power from NorthWestern Energy.

community.notes: Montana energy task force studying data center impacts; NorthWestern Energy's statewide Montana load is ~760 MW total. Multiple proposed MT data centers collectively seeking 1,400 MW more. Northern Plains Resource Council tracking grid and water concerns.

sources.label: EnCap-backed Quantica Details Plans for 1GW Campus in Montana
Quantica Introduces Big Sky Digital Infrastructure as Its Flagship Montana Platform

notes: 5,100-acre site in Yellowstone County between Broadview and Billings ('north of Billings'). Shovel-ready powered land; grid power expected 2027. Interconnection application filed May 2026 for 1,100 MW. Backed by EnCap Investments (~$2.7B under management). Construction planned to begin 2026. Coordinates approximate between Billings and Broadview.

name: Stak Energy North Slope Data Center
poweredBy: On-site natural gas generation (off-grid, new pipeline from North Slope reserves)

statusHistory.note: Alaska DNR issued preliminary best-interest finding supporting long-term state land lease for 715.4 acres; public review process initiated

energy.notes: New pipeline to carry North Slope natural gas to on-site power plant. Self-contained, not connected to Alaska's statewide electrical grid.

water.notes: Arctic air cooling reduces water consumption by 90%+ compared to industry norms per company. Sub-freezing average temperatures enable free air cooling.

community.notes: No confirmed customers or investors as of May 2026. Turbine sourcing delays (industry backlog up to 7 years) cited as major risk. Alaska-native leadership including former state DNR commissioner John Boyle. Indigenous opposition reported.

sources.label: Alaska's North Slope Proposed as Home for Massive AI Data Center
A Huge Data Center Could Rise on Alaska's North Slope

notes: Preliminary state land lease approval covers 715.4 acres (corrected from 640; one square mile = 640 acres exactly but the approved lease footprint is 715.4 acres per Alaska Beacon). Campus is approximately 26 miles south of Deadhorse/Prudhoe Bay off the Dalton Highway (corrected from 25 miles). Supports AI training, cloud computing, and HPC. 3 GW is the project's claimed ultimate potential; $500M is Phase 1 capital. Coordinates estimated (~26 miles south of Prudhoe Bay 70.2N/148.5W); exact site not publicly disclosed.

name: CoreSite Bowers Data Center
poweredBy: Silicon Valley Power (SVP) grid; new on-site substation planned

statusHistory.note: GI Partners assembled Walsh Bowers Technology Center properties for ~$79M; 2805 Bowers Ave acquired from KeyPoint Credit Union for $37.5M

Santa Clara City Council unanimously approved demolition of 1975 office building and construction of 72MW, 244,070 sq ft four-story data center (Bowers Data Center and Bowers Backup Generating Facility); Planning Commission had deadlocked 3-2
CoreSite affiliate acquired 2805 Bowers Ave for ~$57M (part of $100M three-property deal from GI Partners); planning permission carries with land; redevelopment plans expected

sources.label: GI Partners Secures Planning Permission for 72MW Data Center in Santa Clara
CoreSite Acquires Silicon Valley Office Property Zoned for Data Center Redevelopment
2805 Bowers Avenue — Data Center Project Listing

energy.notes: New SVP substation to be constructed on-site. Backup: 32 Tier 4 diesel generators totaling 72MW emergency capacity.

community.notes: Planning Commission voted 3-2 in favor but lacked quorum for formal recommendation. City Council approved unanimously in August 2024, overriding the deadlock. Mayor stated she had 'never heard any opposition to data centers.' No significant organized public opposition recorded.

notes: Coordinates approximate based on 2805 Bowers Ave address. Originally a GI Partners project; CoreSite (owned by American Tower) acquired in Feb 2026 and is expected to redevelop under existing permit. CoreSite already operates a dense interconnection campus nearby in Santa Clara. No construction timeline announced as of mid-2026.

name: Amazon AWS Gilroy Data Center

statusHistory.note: Permit application initially received by City of Gilroy; Amazon purchased 56-acre site for $31.3M in 2020
Gilroy Community Development Director Sharon Goei approved Phase 1 (218,000 sq ft building, 49MW PG&E connection) on July 3, 2025; appeal filed July 21, 2025, withdrawn August 18, 2025
Grading permit issued December 11, 2025; building permit issued March 19, 2026; City of Gilroy project listing shows status as Under Construction

sources.label: Amazon Data Services Gains Approval for Data Center on 56 Acres in Gilroy, California
Gilroy Data Center (by Amazon Web Services) — Official City Project Page
Data Center Set for Construction in East Gilroy
Amazon data center investment: local jobs, education, sustainability
Guest View: What Amazon didn't tell you (Gilroy data center FEIR water figures)

energy.notes: Phase 1: 49MW PG&E grid connection. Potential 50MW Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) on-site. Phase 2 adds a second 218,000 sq ft building with alternative backup power solutions. Total planned capacity ~98MW across both phases.

community.notes: Organized resident opposition active. Concerns cited: utility cost spikes, noise, fire and explosion risk from large-scale lithium BESS, and objection to AI use. An appeal was filed July 21, 2025, subsequently withdrawn August 18, 2025. Opponents requested Amazon guarantee the facility would not be used exclusively for AI workloads.

notes: Status updated to under_construction: grading permit issued December 11, 2025; building permit issued March 19, 2026; City of Gilroy lists project as Under Construction as of July 2026. Site at 8050 Camino Arroyo in east Gilroy along Hwy 101. Full build-out: 438,000 sq ft across 56 acres. Amazon cites a ~$2 billion investment for the Gilroy project (recorded as investmentUsd); its broader Northern California investment of ~$5.1B and ~1,890 jobs since 2009 is regional and not attributable to this site. The 2020 land purchase was $31.3M. Operational job figures conflict and are unconfirmed: the FEIR says employment 'may grow to 50 full-time employees' (a ceiling), the permit application lists 25, and Amazon's promotional materials cite ~90 on-site; the construction workforce is not disclosed.

water.notes: Final EIR-certified use is 23 acre-feet/year total (~7.49 million gallons/yr) at full build-out — 18 AFY for cooling (~0.016 MGD), 3 AFY landscaping, 2 AFY domestic (FEIR p.186); drawn from Gilroy potable supply pending a recycled-water pipeline. Evaporative cooling is triggered only when outdoor temperature exceeds 83F (~3% of the year). Amazon's promotional figure of ~4 million gallons/yr (~36 households) is roughly half the FEIR-certified total and is disputed in a CEQA challenge; Amazon says it aims for 100% reclaimed cooling water by 2030.

name: CyrusOne AI Data Center at NAS Lemoore
poweredBy: Off-grid microgrid (engine generators built by Ameresco; potential 425MW solar farm on adjacent 920 acres)

statusHistory.note: Ameresco, U.S. Navy, and CyrusOne announced public-private partnership to build 100MW AI-optimized data center at NAS Lemoore; first phase expected online 2027; land leased from DOD

sources.label: Ameresco and U.S. Navy Partner to Build AI-Optimized Data Center and Energy Infrastructure at NAS Lemoore
Ameresco Partners with CyrusOne to Develop Offgrid AI Data Center at Naval Air Station Lemoore in California
Groundbreaking for NAS Lemoore data center & energy project
Gilcrease calls for dialogue over data center

energy.notes: Fully off-grid microgrid system including engine generators and control systems built by Ameresco. Aligned with Trump Administration AI Action Plan. Separate discussions ongoing for 425MW solar farm on 920 acres of surrounding DOD land.

notes: Located at Naval Air Station Lemoore on the Kings/Fresno county border. Ameresco leads energy infrastructure via its Intelliscale platform; CyrusOne operates the data center for federal and defense AI workloads (FedRAMP, FISMA High, DoD IL5/IL6). Land leased from DOD. Announced September 9, 2025; first phase expected online 2027.

water.notes: Developer has not disclosed cooling method or water source (arid Central Valley; NAS Lemoore draws from the Kings River). Kings County Supervisor Gilcrease flagged water as a key opposition concern.

community.notes: Kings County Supervisor Garrett Gilcrease (also Farm Bureau president) opposed the project on water grounds during his 2024 campaign; the project advanced with little public input; no formal legal challenge as of mid-2026. A 2023 EIR projects ~400 construction workers and ~20 permanent employees for the proposed facility (projections, not confirmed headcount). The ~$1B figure reported by the Hanford Sentinel covers the integrated project (data center + ~425 MW solar + battery storage), not the data center alone — so no data-center-only investmentUsd.

name: Imperial Valley Computer Manufacturing AI Data Center

statusHistory.note: Imperial County Board of Supervisors voted 4-1 to vacate a public right-of-way and approve a lot merger across 75 acres related to the project site; Chairperson Price subsequently clarified the vote 'did not approve, authorize, or commit to any future development'

Imperial County Board of Supervisors began deliberating an emergency moratorium on new data center approvals; adoption not confirmed as of cited June 4, 2026 source. IVCM entangled in numerous lawsuits including federal civil rights suit against City of Imperial; proceedings regarding IID Colorado River water rights ongoing.

sources.label: California's Largest Data Center Plan Rouses Imperial County
Data Center Moratorium? Imperial County Considers It
AI Data Center Plan Energizes Opposition in California Desert

energy.notes: Would consume approximately twice Imperial County's entire 2024 electricity use. IID rejected water service application for reclaimed water cooling; IVCM filed suit seeking 260 million gallons of Colorado River water annually.

water.notes: Developer requested 750,000 gallons/day of reclaimed water from IID (out of a proposed 6 million gallon daily allocation). IID rejected water service application. IVCM subsequently sued for approximately 260 million gallons per year of Colorado River water.

community.notes: 3,400+ residents signed petition opposing project. Site is adjacent to two residential neighborhoods with hundreds of homes and within 1 mile of schools and playgrounds. City of Imperial filed 231-page court complaint citing noise, hazardous emissions, fire, explosion, and toxin risks. Residents organizing 'Imperial County Data Center Prohibition Act' for November 2026 ballot. Calipatria city council voted to draft a 5-year moratorium. Recall petition filed against County Chairwoman Peggy Price. Developer filed federal civil rights lawsuit against City of Imperial.

notes: Coordinates approximate; actual site is near Aten Road and Clark Road just outside Imperial city limits. Developer Sebastian Rucci (Imperial Valley Computer Manufacturing) proposes ~950,000 sq ft facility described by developer as 'designed exclusively for advanced artificial intelligence and machine learning operations.' Imperial County Board of Supervisors was deliberating an emergency moratorium on new data center approvals as of June 4, 2026 — adoption not confirmed by cited source. Annual promised tax revenue: ~$28M.

name: ECL CSC-1 FlexGrid AI Data Center
poweredBy: FlexGrid hybrid: SVP on-grid electricity + natural gas + hydrogen fuel cells

statusHistory.note: ECL publicly announced CSC-1, a 35MW FlexGrid-powered AI data center in Santa Clara; first phase delivery targeted Q4 2026

sources.label: ECL 35MW FlexGrid-Powered Data Center in Santa Clara Builds Atop On-Grid Power for High-Density AI Delivery
ECL Plans 35MW Data Center in California That Will Use Some Hydrogen Power

energy.notes: FlexGrid architecture combines SVP on-grid electricity with natural gas and hydrogen fuel cells behind the meter, bypassing SVP grid interconnection queue constraints. Sub-1.15 PUE target. Rack densities 75-270kW for AI high-density compute.

water.notes: When hydrogen fuel cells are used, by-product water from hydrogen generation supplies the cooling loop, eliminating freshwater draw — significant for drought-prone California.

notes: Exact street address not publicly disclosed. Coordinates are Santa Clara city centroid (approximate). Announced April 21, 2026. First phase targets Q4 2026 delivery. ECL CEO Yuval Bachar stated the project addresses SVP grid capacity constraints that have forced other data center developers into multi-year interconnection queues. Project targets AI training and inference workloads.

name: Prime Data Centers SMF02

statusHistory.note: Initial Sacramento campus announced; SMF01 (8MW at 2407 AK Street) went operational January 14, 2020 and is fully leased
Prime Data Centers broke ground on SMF02 on May 7, 2026; Clune Construction as general contractor

sources.label: Prime Data Centers Breaks Ground on Second Sacramento Data Center, Expanding Regional Campus Footprint
Prime Breaks Ground on Latest Data Center in Sacramento, California

water.notes: Closed-loop water recirculation system reduces consumption vs. traditional evaporative systems. Water Restoration Certificates procured at 120% of annual operating consumption.

notes: Address: 2408 AK Street, McClellan Park, Sacramento, CA 95652. Coordinates approximate (McClellan Business Park). Adjacent to operational SMF01 (8MW at 2407 AK Street). Combined campus: 26MW across 215,000 sq ft on 8.6 acres. Marketed for hyperscale and AI-ready deployments. Certifications: ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type 2.

name: Servpac MTP Building 2

statusHistory.note: Servpac announced MTP Building 2 expansion at Mililani Technology Park; $13M investment planned to approximately 10x existing 1MW IT capacity of Building 1; as of February 2026 reporting, project was in design phase with construction details not yet disclosed

sources.label: Hawaii-Based Cloud Provider Servpac Expands Data Center

energy.notes: Grid power supplemented by on-site solar (500 solar panels generating approximately 1MWh of energy daily per DCD source — capacity is well below 1MW). Independent power and cooling systems capable of operating if commercial power fails.

water.notes: Closed-loop cooling systems eliminate freshwater consumption entirely.

notes: Located in Mililani Technology Park, Central O'ahu, 10 miles inland and 850 feet above sea level, outside major flood, tsunami, and hurricane zones. Building 2 will approximately 10x the existing 1MW IT capacity of MTP Building 1 (Hawaii's only Uptime Institute Tier IV certified facility). Designed to withstand Category 5 hurricane-force winds. Primary use is enterprise colocation; no AI-specific tenant announced. DCD (February 2026) reports project is in design phase; 'broke ground' claim in original record is unsupported by the cited source. Hawaii's structural barriers (highest US electricity costs, limited grid capacity) make large-scale hyperscale AI builds unlikely; Servpac MTP2 is the only Hawaii project of note found.

name: Meta Prometheus
poweredBy: Nuclear PPAs reportedly with Vistra (Ohio plants) and Oklo (Pike County campus); TerraPower PPA reported but not independently confirmed; on-site gas generation referenced in state permit filings

statusHistory.note: Meta publicly confirms Prometheus at New Albany; described as world's first planned 1 GW data center; expected operational 2026. Baxtel (July 2026) reports some phases already operational with future phases planned — overall 1 GW build-out still under construction.

energy.notes: Nuclear PPAs with Vistra (Ohio nuclear plants) and Oklo (1.2 GW Pike County campus) reported and partially corroborated by SEC filings and news; TerraPower PPA referenced in NBC4i (403 on access this session) but not found in any independently accessible source — treat as unconfirmed. On-site gas generation mentioned in state permit filings per record notes; 200 MW figure and 20-acre power yard not independently confirmed from accessible public sources and removed from this record.

community.notes: New Albany City Council granted 15-year property tax abatement; nearby business owners voiced concern over frequent power outages. Broader New Albany data center tax-break policy contested by state legislators.

sources.label: World's first gigawatt-scale data center coming to Ohio
Meta will use nuclear power for its New Albany A.I. data center
Meta: Prometheus Campus Data Center
Ohio has committed at least $2.3 billion in tax breaks for data centers

notes: Baxtel reports campus as partially operational in 2026 with future phases planned; overall 1 GW build-out still in progress. Site address 1500 Beech Rd NW cited in permit filings but not confirmed from independently accessible sources this session. Nuclear PPA claims partially corroborated (Vistra/Oklo via SEC filings and news; TerraPower unconfirmed). On-site gas generation capacity removed pending accessible source confirmation. County listed as Licking — consistent with Beech Road corridor at Licking/Franklin county boundary per stopohiodatacenters.org.

subsidies.program: Ohio Computer Data Center sales-and-use tax exemption (via Meta operating entity Sidecat LLC); reported ~$600M over roughly 40 years

name: Meta Bowling Green (Project Accordion)

name: Project Cardinal Data Center Campus

statusHistory.note: Pioneer Development LLC submitted annexation, rezoning, and utility applications to City of Yorkville for 1,037-acre, 14-building campus

community.notes: Two active lawsuits as of July 2026. (1) John Bryan (filed Oct 28, 2025): a conditional settlement was before the June 23, 2026 City Council — terms limited to a phasing-order revision (NE-SE-W instead of NE-W-SE), no monetary payment to Bryan. (2) Preserve Our Yorkville & Community LLC (filed April 2026): broader suit alleging inadequate annexation/rezoning notice and deviation from the 2016 Comprehensive Plan; still ongoing as of June 30, 2026. Pioneer Development also sought an 18-month extension (to Dec 31, 2027) to close on all parcels — failure voids the annexation agreement. A $51M developer impact-fee package flows FROM the developer TO the city (schools/fire/projects), not a subsidy; no DCEO certification found and Illinois paused its Data Center Investment Program on July 1, 2026.

Planned MW capacity not confirmed from primary sources; 444 MW figure removed as unsupported. Iowa's Sec. 423.3(95) data-center sales-tax exemption applies, but Iowa data-center incentive awards are not publicly disclosed; no Ginger West-specific job count is published.

water.notes: Microsoft's West Des Moines datacenters use direct evaporative cooling (water ~10% of the year, air-cooled the rest); a planned Ginger West building is committed to next-generation 'zero-water' cooling under WDM's 2023 policy favoring reduced peak water use. Microsoft's WDM fleet (five campuses) used ~70M gallons in 2024 (fleet-wide, not Ginger West-specific); Microsoft funded ~$25M in WDM Water Works improvements incl. a 2M-gallon tank.

community.notes: WDM Mayor Russ Trimble has praised Microsoft as a 'game changer' and 'great corporate citizens' (never exceeding agreed water allotments); neighboring residents cite light pollution, traffic, noise, and water-use concerns. No formal opposition group or litigation identified.
