name: Hanton City Business Park (data center anchor)

statusHistory.note: Hanton City Investments LLC (Ryan Polumbo of Revity Energy and John Branca) filed a zoning application for an industrial/warehousing park with a data center anchor tenant on undeveloped woodland near the Fidelity Investments campus. The application was filed May 4, 2026 — one day before the Smithfield Town Council voted 4-1 to ban data centers. No megawatt capacity has been publicly disclosed.

community.notes: Smithfield Town Council voted 4-1 (May 5, 2025) to amend zoning to list data centers as "not permitted" in all districts; Hanton City Investments filed its application May 4 — one day before the vote — preserving pre-ban review (a use variance faces "a pretty high bar"). Council President Tassoni expects the developer to sue. The widely cited $59M is projected 30-year town tax REVENUE, not facility investment; the developer said the project "may never happen" absent a state tax break (RI SB 2346, a 30-year sales/use exemption, was held by the Senate as of April 2026).

sources.label: Proposed data center still looms over Smithfield, even after town council enacts ban
Proposed data center still looms over Smithfield after ban
Smithfield puts the brakes on potential data center

notes: No megawatt capacity has been publicly disclosed, so capacityMw is omitted. The $59M figure cited in coverage is projected town tax revenue over 30 years, not facility investment, so investmentUsd is not set. Additional coverage (Rhode Island Current, ecoRI News) returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch and is not cited; the Ocean State Media article is the single independently verified resolvable source. Coordinates are approximate Smithfield-area coordinates.

name: Sioux Falls Technology Park (Gemini Data Center)
poweredBy: Grid (Xcel Energy; adjacent to Xcel's Split Rock substation)

statusHistory.note: Gemini Data Center SD LLC (a California family office under Michael Anvar) acquired ~164 acres from Xcel Energy northeast of Sioux Falls, adjacent to Xcel's Split Rock substation, and sought city approvals for a data center supporting up to 500 MW. Gemini is the land developer, not the operator; no tenant is signed.

Sioux Falls City Council approved annexation (October 8, 2025) and unanimously approved rezoning from agricultural to light industrial (January 2026), despite hours of opposing testimony. Construction was targeted for 2026 and operation for 2028. The developer committed to funding all utility extensions at its own cost.

energy.notes: Site adjacent to Xcel's Split Rock substation and Angus Anson power station; an Xcel facility study is in process. The developer committed to funding utility extensions and substation upgrades.

community.notes: The city council approved rezoning unanimously despite hours of opposing public testimony; a citizen petition drive is gathering signatures that could force the rezoning to a public vote. A state 50-year sales-tax exemption bill failed in the 2026 legislative session.

sources.label: Hyperscale data center seeking city approvals to build northeast of Sioux Falls
Sioux Falls data center developers detail next steps
Planned hyperscale data center moving into South Dakota's largest city

notes: aiClassification is 'likely' (not 'confirmed'): Gemini is the land developer, no tenant is signed, and only developer aspiration to attract a hyperscale/AI/neocloud tenant is on record. Capacity (500 MW) and acreage per SiouxFalls.Business; an earlier report cited 160 acres. Coordinates are approximate for the northeast Sioux Falls site. As a developer land project with no signed tenant, its water, subsidy, investment, and job figures remain developer projections only (South Dakota has no state income tax and rejected data-center incentive bills); all recorded as honest-zeros pending a confirmed tenant.

name: Applied Digital Deuel County AI Campus

statusHistory.note: Applied Digital proposed a 430 MW AI data center campus near Toronto, SD, with up to $16 billion of announced investment and up to 200 permanent jobs. Data Center Dynamics reported the company was 'proposing an AI data center development east of Toronto in South Dakota.'

Applied Digital told Keloland (statement March 2026) that the project was 'unlikely to go forward,' citing the South Dakota legislature's failure to pass a 50-year sales-tax exemption on IT equipment, and said it would let its land option near Toronto lapse.

energy.notes: Proposed site was near Otter Tail Power transmission lines and a natural gas plant in Astoria.

sources.label: Applied Digital proposes multi-billion-dollar data center in South Dakota
Applied Digital says data center near Toronto, South Dakota, unlikely to go forward

notes: Status is 'cancelled' per Applied Digital's own March 2026 statement (land option to lapse). The 'up to $16 billion' investment and up to 200 jobs were announced figures for the proposal and are recorded in statusHistory rather than as structured investmentUsd/jobs, since the project will not proceed. aiClassification is 'confirmed' — Data Center Dynamics reported it as an AI data center. The Otter Tail Power association reflects the proposed (now abandoned) siting.

name: AWS Project Green Campus

statusHistory.note: Application filed via NorthPoint Development, LLC for a 1,000-acre parcel at the northeast quadrant of I-70 and Highway 19 interchange near New Florence; two-phase campus of up to 21 data center buildings.

Montgomery County Commission unanimously approved the project and tax abatement incentive framework in December 2025; public safety entities (ambulance district, fire protection district) opted out of Chapter 100 participation and retained full tax allocations.

Construction under way by April 2026; Preserve Montgomery County LLC lawsuit filed in February 2026 pending. Governor Kehoe and AWS made the official public $10B investment announcement on June 15, 2026.

sources.label: Amazon announces $10B Montgomery County data center plan
Amazon commits $10bn to data center campus in Montgomery County, Missouri
1,000-acre AWS data center campus under consideration in Montgomery County, Missouri
AWS new facilities near New Florence will double tax revenue for Montgomery County
New Florence AWS data center to tap local aquifer for water
Montgomery County approves land for two large-scale projects on Amazon data center campus
Amazon Project Green Campus — Baxtel listing

notes: Codename 'Project Green'; application filed via NorthPoint Development, LLC. Two-phase build-out: Phase 1 = 8 data center buildings; Phase 2 = 13 additional buildings (21 total). Located on ~1,000 acres at northeast quadrant of I-70 and Highway 19 interchange near New Florence. Official AWS/Governor Kehoe announcement on June 15, 2026 cited a $10B investment commitment. Earlier county planning documents and initial filings referenced a maximum capital investment ceiling of up to $35B — that figure is a speculative ceiling, not a committed amount. AWS separately announced a 138MW renewable energy project in Missouri; not confirmed as dedicated to this campus. Community investments included in announcement: $3M for county 911/emergency dispatch upgrades, $3M for community programs, $1M for Montgomery County Fairgrounds, $150K community fund, $50K teacher grant. Preserve Montgomery County LLC filed suit in February 2026 alleging ten counts of Missouri Sunshine Law violations (closed-session negotiations, NDAs with Amazon); case pending as of mid-2026. First buildings targeted operational by 2027; full campus potentially complete ~2032. Coordinates are approximate (parcel centroid at I-70 / Hwy 19 NE quadrant). Jobs: KOMU reported ~400 permanent jobs; DCD's coverage of the same $10B announcement cited no specific permanent figure (only 'thousands of construction jobs') — permanent count left unstructured pending firm corroboration.

energy.notes: Powered by Ameren Missouri grid. AWS committed to working with Ameren to ensure project energy costs are not passed to other ratepayers. AWS announced a separate 138MW renewable energy project in Missouri (not confirmed as on-site or dedicated to this campus).

water.notes: Water sourced from Cambrian-Ordovician aquifer via wells drilled to ~1,500 ft depth (to avoid impacting local private wells at shallower depths). Initial phase projected at 2.9M gallons/year; full 17-building campus projected at ~50M gallons/year (~0.137 MGD equivalent). Regional aquifer capacity exceeds 23 trillion gallons; current regional utilization ~8%; this project adds ~0.03%. On-site stormwater management ponds and water treatment plant planned. Cooling methodology not publicly specified.

subsidies.program: Montgomery County Chapter 100 bond / PILOT — personal property tax abatements on declining schedule approved December 2025; real property taxes paid in full; public safety districts (ambulance, fire) opted out and retain full allocations. Total multi-year abatement value not publicly disclosed.

community.notes: Preserve Montgomery County LLC (coalition of local residents, farmers, and business owners) filed a 10-count lawsuit in February 2026 alleging Missouri Sunshine Law violations — specifically that county commissioners conducted negotiations in closed sessions and signed NDAs with Amazon. Community concerns at public meetings included water usage impacts, potential electric rate increases, and loss of rural character. Some residents and officials expressed support for the project's economic benefits. Construction proceeded despite the pending suit. Montgomery County Commission stated real property taxes are not abated and projected the project would double the county's tax revenue at full buildout.

name: Project Matador

statusHistory.note: Fermi America founded January 2025 by Rick Perry, Toby Neugebauer, and Griffin Perry. Project Matador announced on ~5,769-acre site in Carson County under 99-year lease with Texas Tech University System, adjacent to DOE Pantex Plant.

600MW+ natural gas turbines acquired across two deals (July 2025); COLA for four AP1000 reactors filed with NRC June 17 2025; NRC accepted COLA for review September 2025; TCEQ preliminary approval for first 6GW gas generation (November 2025); geotechnical work commenced on campus.

sources.label: Fermi America acquires 600MW of gas turbines for 11GW AI mega campus in Amarillo, Texas
Fermi America Makes Initial Draw on $200M Equipment Facility to Accelerate 2.3GW Delivery
Fermi America and State of Texas Announce TCEQ Preliminary Approval for First 6GW Natural Gas Facilities
Fermi America Project Matador AP1000 Units 1-4 Combined License Application
Fermi America looks to go public as NRC accepts COLA for AP1000s
Project Matador: Massive AI Data Center in Texas Panhandle Promises Pollution
Rick Perry's AI power startup Fermi already has a $16 billion market cap—and zero revenue
Financially struggling, AI power startup Fermi loses its CEO and CFO for '2.0' reset

notes: 11GW is the long-term ceiling for the HyperGrid campus; near-term company targets include 1GW by end of 2026 and 2.3GW first-phase delivery — these are company goals, not firm commitments. Land acreage reported variously (5,769 acres per DCD July 2025 and 5,855 acres per other sources; later company materials cite 7,570 acres on 7,570 acres); using 5,769 as the earliest confirmed figure, sourced from the gas turbine acquisition announcement. Coordinates approximate (seed-provided; site in Carson County ~12 miles NE of Amarillo, adjacent to DOE Pantex Plant). Fermi America co-founded January 2025 by former Texas Governor and U.S. Energy Secretary Rick Perry, Toby Neugebauer (CEO, departed April 2026 after being fired 'without cause'), and Griffin Perry. IPO raised $682M on Nasdaq under ticker FRMI (October 2025), valuing company at ~$19B at opening; market cap collapsed to ~$3.4B by April 2026 after leadership exits and loss of anchor tenant ($150M LOI canceled December 2025). Company organized as a REIT; had zero revenue at IPO. Investment: equipment financing commitments exceed $600M ($500M MUFG turbine warehouse + $200M Keystone/Cape facility); projected total project cost $70–90B is a range/projection, not a firm figure — omitted from investmentUsd per data-rigor policy. Jobs: 50,000+ projected across campus lifetime — projection only, omitted from jobs field. Nuclear campus name in NRC COLA filings: 'President Donald J. Trump Advanced Energy and Intelligence Campus'; publicly marketed as Project Matador / HyperGrid.

energy.notes: Initial generation: on-site natural gas. Fermi acquired 600MW+ of gas turbines in two deals (July 2025): 478MW from Firebird LNG/Siemens Energy (six SGT800 turbines + heat recovery steam generators + one SST600 steam turbine) and 135MW from GE 6B Frame units acquired from a New Jersey facility. TCEQ issued preliminary approval for up to 6GW of combined-cycle gas (November 2025); air permit secured February 2026 after ~279 public comments. Long-term plan adds four Westinghouse AP1000 reactors (~4.4GW nuclear baseload; COLA filed June 17 2025, NRC accepted two of three parts for review September 2025, Part 3 deadline December 31 2026, first reactor targeted 2031, all four by 2038) plus solar and battery storage. Fermi is also evaluating SMRs (MOU with Doosan Enerbility). Entire campus is behind-the-meter — private grid, no public utility offtake.

water.notes: No confirmed water use figures published. Community opposition raised concerns about Ogallala Aquifer impacts from combined cooling demand of gas plants and data centers. Arid Texas Panhandle location heightens water supply risk.

community.notes: TCEQ air permit process (for 93 planned gas turbines) drew 279+ public comments, majority opposed. 63 contested case hearing requests filed before State Office of Administrative Hearings; 21 additional public meeting requests. Primary opposition group: Panhandle Taxpayers for Transparency. Key concerns: air quality (modeled 2,140 tpy CO and 1,591 tpy NOx), water demand, impacts on farmland and nearby cattle operations (Tyson Foods facility adjacent). Fermi bused in college students to a December 2025 public meeting, stoking further controversy.

name: Goodnight Campus

statusHistory.note: Armstrong County Commissioners Court approved tax abatement agreement with GN DC1, LLC (Crusoe Energy entity) at November 12, 2024 public hearing. Tenant not publicly named at that time.

Google confirmed partnership with Crusoe for Goodnight campus as part of $40B statewide Texas announcement, November 2025. Satellite imagery (Cleanview, Feb 24 2026) confirmed two buildings under active construction with roof installation and steel framing underway.

sources.label: Google commits $40bn to expand cloud and AI infrastructure in Texas through 2027
Google to Use Natural Gas to Power Massive Data Center in Texas
Google Is Considering Natural Gas Without Carbon Capture in Its AI Data Center Strategy
How a Crusoe-Google campus became Texas' co-location test case
Google Launches 1-GW-Plus Co-Located Data Center and Generation Complex in Texas Panhandle
Armstrong County AI data center project scaled back from original plan
Tax Abatement Agreement between Armstrong County, Texas and GN DC1, LLC (Phase 1)

notes: Campus name: Goodnight, near the town of Claude, TX (known address: 10001 Lima Rd, Claude, TX 79019). Coordinates approximate (based on dispatch seed; county parcel area). Developed by Crusoe Energy via entity GN DC1, LLC (Delaware LLC, 255 Filmore St., Denver, CO 80206); Google is the hyperscale tenant/customer, confirmed publicly November 2025. Tax abatement agreement was signed with GN DC1, LLC in late 2024 without publicly naming the tenant. Google confirmed the Crusoe partnership but had not signed an offtake agreement for the on-site gas plant as of early 2026; negotiations on power amounts still ongoing per Google spokesperson. Planned IT capacity: Phase 1 described in tax abatement agreement as 'anticipated to consume up to 530 MW,' 4 buildings each with 4 x 25 MW data halls, on approximately 350 acres; Phase 2 (possible additional 3+ buildings) reported at up to ~500 MW additional; combined full build-out reported as 1 GW+ — all capacity figures are 'up to' ceilings from permit and abatement documents, not firm contracted amounts. Project was scaled back from an original 7-building plan (October 2025 local reporting); 4 buildings with construction permits from TDLR as of early 2026; Crusoe described 3-4 buildings as likely; grading on additional parcels suggests total program may reach 5+ buildings. First phase expected online 2026; initial county PILOT payment expected June/July 2026. The $40B Google Texas investment is a statewide commitment through 2027 covering multiple sites (Armstrong County, Haskell County, Ellis County) with no site-specific investment figure disclosed by Google. A $29B campus investment figure appeared in Aterio/Crusoe reporting — this is a projected ceiling, not a firm committed amount; omitted from investmentUsd per data-rigor policy. Distinct from Google's separate Haskell County TX site and Red Oak (Ellis County) TX sites — do not conflate.

energy.notes: Crusoe Energy filed TCEQ air construction permit (TCEQ announcement Feb 5, 2026) for a 933 MW natural gas power plant on-site at the Goodnight campus. Gas plant operates off-grid with no ERCOT grid connection for this generation asset. No carbon capture technology. Estimated emissions up to 4.5 million tons CO2/year and 1,600 tons criteria air pollutants/year per permit application. A portion of the campus also co-locates behind the meter with Goodnight Wind farm (265.5 MW, Serena Energy, came online 2024) under Texas SB6 net metering arrangement; proposal submitted to Public Utility Commission of Texas (PUCT), regulatory review ongoing as of mid-2026. Separate TCEQ wastewater permit WQ0016881001 filed under same entity, indicating water/utility infrastructure planning underway.

water.notes: No water volume or cooling system type disclosed in sources reviewed. TCEQ wastewater permit WQ0016881001 filed under the same GN DC1 LLC entity, indicating water infrastructure is being permitted; no public details available on cooling approach or projected water consumption.

subsidies.program: Armstrong County Tax Abatement Agreement (Chapter 312, Texas Tax Code) with GN DC1, LLC — ad valorem property tax abatement up to 10 years on eligible improvements; amended 2025 to per-building Payments in Lieu of Taxes (PILOTs) of $1.35M/year/building once operational; original structure was $5.4M/year per phase ($10.8M/year at full build-out) for 10 years; county attorney noted total money would not change under amendment

community.notes: Armstrong County Commissioners Court approved the original tax abatement agreement unanimously at the November 12, 2024 public hearing. The amended PILOT structure was also approved unanimously in 2025 after hearing from elected officials from multiple taxing entities and members of the public. County Judge Adam Ensey publicly supported the amendment, noting it allows revenue collection to begin with each completed building rather than waiting for phase completion. Initial county payment expected June/July 2026.

name: IREN Horizon — Childress, TX

statusHistory.note: Horizon 1 (75 MW gross / 50 MW IT) announced March 11, 2025; construction underway at existing Childress campus
IREN signs $9.7B five-year Microsoft GPU cloud contract November 2025; Horizons 1–4 at Childress targeted for end-2026 full deployment
Horizon 1 (50 MW IT / 75 MW gross) energized Q4 2025 — IREN's first purpose-built AI data center; Horizons 2–4 remain under construction

sources.label: IREN plans 75MW liquid-cooled AI data center in Texas
Childress Data Center — 750MW AI-Optimised Facility
IREN Secures $9.7bn AI Cloud Contract with Microsoft
IREN: Horizon 1 Data Center
IREN continues developing data center campus in Childress
Microsoft signs $9.7bn cloud capacity deal with IREN

notes: SEPARATE SITE from IREN Sweetwater (Nolan/Fisher County — do not conflate). Full 750 MW figure is total campus transmission capacity; the AI/Microsoft Horizon 1–4 build-out comprises four 50 MW IT (75 MW gross) liquid-cooled data halls = 200 MW IT total, targeting end-2026 delivery. Horizon 1 (50 MW IT / 75 MW gross) energized Q4 2025 — operational; Horizons 2–4 remain under construction as of mid-2026. Coordinates approximate (county seat area). Site is a 576-acre freehold parcel with on-site 345 kV substations connecting directly to ERCOT. Investment for Horizon 1–4 reported as $300–350M (range; not a firm figure — omitted from investmentUsd). Microsoft contract ($9.7B total, 5-year through ~2031) covers NVIDIA GB300 Blackwell Ultra GPU cloud services; 20% upfront prepayment funded capex. Dell Technologies hardware supply agreement: $5.8B. IREN also holds a separate NVIDIA cloud services contract at Childress. Rack density 130–200 kW per rack; projected PUE 1.05–1.10. Approximately 25 current full-time local employees, 50+ expected at 750 MW full build-out; over 500 contractors during construction phase (approximate reported figures — omitted from jobs numeric fields). IREN Community Grants Program distributes $250,000 annually to the Childress area.

energy.notes: Direct 345 kV grid connection via on-site substations owned by IREN. IREN states it utilizes abundant low-cost renewable energy available on ERCOT and actively curtails consumption during high-demand periods. No on-site gas generation confirmed for this site.

water.notes: Direct-to-chip closed-loop liquid cooling. IREN states the system does not consume water on an ongoing basis; annual top-ups require roughly as much water as a few houses. Water is trucked in externally — deliberately not drawn from Childress municipal supply. System described as analogous to a vehicle radiator.

community.notes: Local leadership and community described as strongly supportive. IREN COO stated community partnership was essential to construction progress. Community Grants Program distributes $250,000 annually to Childress area. IREN committed to local hiring and contracting with local businesses where possible.

name: IREN Sweetwater Campus

statusHistory.note: Jan 21, 2025 IREN business update: first formal public announcement of 1,400 MW Sweetwater data center development. October 2024 update had disclosed HPC/AI pivot on 1,300+ acres.
Mar 17, 2025: IREN announces 600 MW grid connection agreement for Sweetwater 2 in Fisher County, bringing total secured power to 2.75 GW across West Texas.
May 1, 2026: Sweetwater 1 1,400 MW substation energized, connecting to ERCOT grid via 345kV high-voltage line. Data centers commissioning in phases; power to ramp with construction.

sources.label: IREN Announces Successful Energization of Sweetwater 1
IREN Announces Successful Energization of Sweetwater 1 (May 1, 2026)
IREN Business Update Jan 21, 2025 — first 1,400 MW Sweetwater announcement
IREN Secures 2.75GW for Data Centers in West Texas — Sweetwater 2 600 MW announcement (Mar 17, 2025)
IREN Secures $9.7bn AI Cloud Contract with Microsoft (Nov 3, 2025)
Iren energizes first phase of 2GW Sweetwater data center campus in Texas
IREN Expands Data Center Power to 2.75GW with New Texas Agreement (Sweetwater 2)
NVIDIA Anchors IREN With $3.4B Contract And $2.1B Equity Warrant (May 2026)
Nolan County approves Tax Increment Reinvestment Zone No. 1 for hyperscale data center development (June 2025)
Rural Texas casting skeptical eye on data center openings — water concerns (Oct 2025)
IREN Sweetwater 1 — interconnection queue record

notes: Two-site campus: Sweetwater 1 (1,400 MW, ~1,700 acres, Nolan/Jones County boundary area) substation energized May 1, 2026 via 345kV ERCOT connection; data centers commissioning in phases. Sweetwater 2 (600 MW, ~500 acres, Fisher County, near Hamlin TX, ~28 miles from SW1) targeted energization late 2027 per IREN (some sources cite 2028). Direct fiber loop planned between sites for single 2 GW compute hub with <10ms latency to Dallas. Coordinates are approximate (Sweetwater city center, Nolan County seat). County boundary sources conflicting: DCD says Fisher County for both sites; interconnection.fyi and dispatch brief cite Nolan County for SW1; northwiseproject.com says Nolan and Jones Counties span the campus. IREN's $9.7B Microsoft contract (Nov 2025) and $3.4B NVIDIA contract (May 2026) are company-wide deals currently deployed at Childress, TX campus (Horizon 1-4); the NVIDIA partnership press release specifically names Sweetwater as the flagship future deployment site for NVIDIA DSX factory architecture. No confirmed customer contracts specific to Sweetwater site as of 2026-07-07. IREN was in Sweetwater since 2022 as a Bitcoin mining site; HPC/AI pivot announced October 2024. Interconnection.fyi lists renewable energy sourcing via RECs (wind/solar) at this site.

energy.notes: 345kV high-voltage connection to ERCOT grid. IREN and third-party sources describe West Texas as one of the world's largest renewable energy markets; interconnection.fyi lists renewable energy via RECs (wind/solar) for this site.

water.notes: Direct-to-chip liquid cooling plus supplemental air cooling. Closed-loop design described by IREN and analysts as dramatically reducing water consumption vs. traditional evaporative approaches. West Texas arid climate noted as a water-conservation design driver. No reported MGD figure confirmed.

community.notes: No documented site-specific opposition to IREN Sweetwater found. Nolan County TIRZ public hearing (June 2025) recorded zero opposition comments. Broader rural West Texas context: Texas Tribune (Oct 2025) reported growing community concern over data center water use; Texas PUC water survey (June 2026) found most operators non-responsive. TIRZ was approved by Nolan County to enable hyperscale data center development; specific operator in TIRZ article is Deriva Energy (ROW agreement), not confirmed as IREN.

name: Meta Project Anthem — Tulsa

statusHistory.note: Preliminary plat filed with Tulsa Metropolitan Area Planning Commission as 'Project Anthem'; Meta identity not publicly confirmed at time of filing
Preliminary plat approved August 7, 2024 by TMAPC; developer entity listed as Atmoss LLC
Meta publicly confirmed as operator; groundbreaking ceremony held April 21, 2026 with Oklahoma Governor Kevin Stitt; completion targeted 2028

sources.label: Breaking Ground on a New AI-Optimized Data Center in Tulsa, Oklahoma
Hello, Tulsa! — Meta Data Centers
Meta Breaks Ground on New $1 Billion Data Center in Tulsa
Meta's east Tulsa data center earns praise and protests
340-acre data center campus planned in Tulsa, Oklahoma
Gov. Stitt announces Meta behind Tulsa data center known as 'Project Anthem'

notes: Codename: Project Anthem (used in TMAPC filings; Meta confirmed publicly April 21, 2026). Meta's 28th US data center and 32nd globally; first in Oklahoma and first built within Tulsa city limits. Anchor tenant at Fair Oaks Innovation Park (a 2,000-acre campus) near 11th Street and Creek Turnpike, East Tulsa. Investment stated as 'more than $1 billion' by Meta — a floor figure only; PartnerTulsa estimates construction costs at ~$800M. investmentUsd omitted: no firm single confirmed figure. Facility size: 2+ million sq ft; completion targeted 2028; general contractor Fortis Construction. No facility-specific MW capacity disclosed; Meta separately adding 1,500+ MW of clean energy to the Oklahoma grid statewide. Jobs: Meta states 'more than 1,000 construction workers at peak' and 'approximately 100 permanent jobs' — approximations from press release, not firm figures; omitted from numeric fields. Phase 2 expansion (375 additional acres west of original parcel, developer Atmoss LLC) proposed but withdrawn March 2026 before city council vote. Coordinates approximate (near 11th St & Creek Turnpike, East Tulsa).

energy.notes: Meta committed to matching 100% of facility electricity use with clean energy added to the Oklahoma grid. Existing Oklahoma projects add 1,500+ MW of clean energy statewide (not facility-specific). Meta covers full energy costs directly via PSO utility bills; stated commitment to avoid ratepayer burden. Tulsa Metropolitan Utility Authority also listed as a utility partner (water/wastewater).

water.notes: Water-efficient closed-loop liquid-cooled system. Meta states zero water use for the majority of the year and a water-positive goal by 2030. Water restoration partnership with Phytech across ~1,500 acres targeting 50+ million gallons of annual water savings. Additional partnership with Tulsa Community Foundation on water stewardship. No reportedMgd figure confirmed.

subsidies.program: Tax Incentive District (TID) — 85% annual property tax exemption; 15% collected projected at ~$36 million to taxing entities (city of Tulsa, Wagoner County, Catoosa Public Schools, others) over 25-year incentive period

community.notes: Tulsa City Council passed a 9-month moratorium on new data centers in March 2026 to evaluate ecological impact; Project Anthem was carved out as it was approved before the moratorium vote. Approximately a dozen protesters from Stop Data Colonialism coalition gathered at the April 21, 2026 groundbreaking, raising concerns about environmental impact, financial burden on nearby residents, and 'data colonialism.' Meta's water-neutrality claims challenged by protesters as corporate narrative. Councilor Christian Bengel voted for the moratorium while separately supporting Project Anthem. Meta announced $3 million in community grants (Eastside Rise neighborhood enhancement, Data Center Community Action Grants) and workforce development partnerships with Tulsa Tech and Tulsa Community College targeting 200+ graduates annually in technical trades.

name: Nebius Vineland Data Center

statusHistory.note: Nebius and DataOne announce phased AI data center in Vineland, NJ; 20-week first-phase build target, total ceiling 'up to 300 MW'

First phase completed in approximately 20 weeks (summer 2025); Nebius committed to 100 MW installed capacity by year-end 2025; Microsoft GPU capacity deliveries begin late 2025. Full campus buildout targeting November 2026.

sources.label: Nebius accelerates US expansion, adding up to 300 MW capacity at new data center in New Jersey
We're introducing the 300 MW New Jersey region and expanding to Iceland
DataOne to build Nebius data center in New Jersey
Vineland residents are protesting AI data center as concerns mount over emissions
A Town Hall Too Late – Vineland Data Center
South Jersey's largest data center is being built in Vineland. Some residents say they've had no say in the process.

notes: Phase-1 (~100 MW) completed summer 2025 in approximately 20 weeks from March 2025 announcement; full campus buildout targeting November 2026. Total design capacity cited as 'up to 300 MW' in Nebius press releases (sourceRef 0); DataOne CEO and local/community coverage consistently cite 350 MW; long-term campus potential described as approaching 1 GW. Coordinates are approximate (site: South Lincoln and Sheridan Avenues, East Vineland; Baxtel lists 805 Sheridan Ave). DataOne is the developer/builder — BSO's data center unit (launched 2024) backed by Ardian; CEO Charles-Antoine Beyney; this is DataOne's first US site (previously Grenoble and Lyon, France). Nebius is the ~10-year tenant; Microsoft GPU infrastructure agreement commencing late 2025 under multi-year AI infrastructure deal. Facility is 100% privately funded; DataOne declined a $6.2M city equipment loan amid community backlash. Distinct from Nebius's Independence, MO (Kansas City) site. NJDEP air permit pending as of early 2026; construction proceeded ahead of final permit issuance. DataOne stated 200+ permanent full-time jobs; construction job count not disclosed.

energy.notes: 32 high-efficiency natural gas engines generate approximately 85% of facility power; 6 diesel generators for backup. Proposed 1.5-million-gallon LNG storage tank as pipeline contingency (pending approval). NJDEP air permit under review as of early 2026; Sierra Club and Pinelands Alliance objecting to emissions profile. Facility located in Wellhead Protected Area near Kirkwood-Cohansey aquifer.

water.notes: Developer explicitly states facility will not consume water for equipment cooling (Philadelphia Inquirer, Jan 2026). A four-step exhaust reclamation system on the gas engines (selective catalytic reduction, oxy catalyst, wet scrubbers, biofilters) captures condensate from engine exhaust for recycling — this is emissions control on the power plant side, not IT-equipment cooling. City department figures show 1.1 million gallons/year municipal water draw (Inquirer, Jan 2026); a separate source (WHYY) cites 'up to 20 million gallons annually' — discrepancy unresolved, likely reflecting different accounting (total throughput vs. net municipal draw). Reporting 'reportedMgd' field omitted due to conflicting figures.

subsidies.program: 5-year PILOT (Payment in Lieu of Taxes), Ordinance 2026-3 — dollar amount not publicly disclosed

community.notes: City council and mayor formally support the project; PILOT Ordinance 2026-3 adopted unanimously January 27, 2026. Approximately 100 residents rallied in opposition (noise, emissions, water, property values, health concerns). Pinelands Alliance hosted community town hall. Sierra Club and Sustain South Jersey formally oppose, citing construction without final environmental permits, proximity to Pinelands, and lack of public process. NJDEP reviewing air permit and investigating facility's emissions as a single-source with adjacent parcels.

name: PowerHouse 95
operator: PowerHouse Data Centers (American Real Estate Partners / AREP, joint venture with Harrison Street)

statusHistory.note: PowerHouse announced completed purchase of 145 acres in Spotsylvania County along I-95/Hwy 17 corridor for PowerHouse 95 campus; three 300 MW substations planned.
Groundbreaking announced; company posted on LinkedIn 'Construction on PH 95 North is underway!' DCD coverage July 19, 2024.

sources.label: PowerHouse breaks ground on 800MW data center campus in Spotsylvania County, Virginia
PowerHouse Data Centers announces purchase of 145 acres in Spotsylvania, VA for PowerHouse 95 campus

notes: Campus located at approximately 4189 Turner Ct, Spotsylvania County, VA along the I-95/Hwy 17 corridor; coordinates are approximate. Three 300 MW substations planned (800 MW total); first substation delivering 150 MW targeted for Q4 2025. Up to 8 buildings, 3.5 million sq ft. Phased delivery Q3 2025–Q4 2027. Joint venture between PowerHouse/AREP and Harrison Street. Harrison Street managing director explicitly cited 'high-powered computing, AI, and other advancements' as the demand driver for this campus class, supporting likely AI classification. Named tenant not disclosed. Dominion Energy utility attribution is inferred from service territory: Dominion's Tributary 230 kV project was approved by Virginia SCC (June 30, 2024) specifically to serve growing Spotsylvania data center demand; PH95 press materials do not name the utility directly. Investment amount, job figures, and water draw not disclosed in any sourced press materials.

energy.notes: Utility inferred from geographic service area and Dominion's Tributary 230 kV transmission project (SCC Final Order June 30, 2024) built specifically to serve Spotsylvania County data center demand. PH95-specific press materials do not name the utility. Rappahannock Electric Cooperative also serves parts of Spotsylvania County and cannot be ruled out.

water.notes: Press materials do not disclose cooling approach for PH 95 specifically. PowerHouse has deployed direct-to-chip and closed-loop cooling at other campuses; no water draw figures available.

community.notes: Spotsylvania County has experienced significant data center controversy: the board voted 4-3 in December 2025 to approve design standards; required special use permits for data centers in February 2026 (mirroring Loudoun, Prince William, and Henrico); public comments cited noise, grid strain, water supply risk, and visual impact. One project (Hunters Ridge South, 2.2M sq ft) passed 5-2 in August 2025. PowerHouse 95 itself was not identified as the specific target of organized opposition in sourced materials — it appears to have proceeded under earlier by-right industrial zoning rules. Rappahannock Electric Cooperative CEO told Spotsylvania officials that one data center can require as much energy as REC's entire customer base during peak demand.

name: QTS Bessemer (Project Marvel)

statusHistory.note: Project Marvel first surfaced March 2025; unnamed developer (via Logistics Land Investment LLC, an affiliate of Atlanta-based TPA Group) sought rezoning of ~700 acres in Bessemer for hyperscale data center
Bessemer City Council voted 5-2 to approve rezoning ~700 acres from agricultural (A-1) to light industrial (I-1) on November 18, 2025; developer identity still not publicly disclosed

Bessemer City Council approved rezoning of 914 additional acres in April 2026, expanding total site to ~1,607 acres; 18-building count unchanged, additional land used for wider buffers and layout flexibility
QTS Data Centers (Blackstone) publicly confirmed as operator behind Project Marvel, July 1-2, 2026; project described as in active planning stage

sources.label: QTS confirms plans for proposed data center campus in Bessemer
Purported Developer of $14bn Project Marvel Data Center in Bessemer Unveiled as QTS
Bessemer City Council approves data center project despite transparency concerns (November 2025)
Bessemer approves rezoning of 914 acres for Project Marvel data center
Alabama's planned $14bn Project Marvel data center could double in size
QTS Data Centers behind Bessemer Project Marvel plans
How Will Project Marvel Affect Bessemer And Surrounding Areas?
Bessemer Residents Are Speaking Out Against AI Data Center Development

notes: Codename: Project Marvel. 18-building hyperscale campus on Red Ridge near I-459 and I-20/59 interchange in Bessemer (Jefferson County). Announced capacity: up to 1,200 MW — reported as a planned ceiling, representing roughly 9% of Alabama Power's current generating capacity; left out of capacityMw field as sources use 'up to' framing. Investment: reported at $14–14.5B across sources (Logistics Land Investment anticipated 'close to $14.4B'; WBRC reported $14.5B at November 2025 rezoning; DCD reported ~$14B); no single firm company-confirmed figure exists — treated as a projected cost range and excluded from investmentUsd. Land: ~1,607.42 acres total after two rezoning rounds (initial ~700 acres November 2025 + 914-acre expansion April 2026). QTS affiliate purchased land from TPA Group affiliate for $431.73M. Chapter 9B statutory eligibility would have provided a 30-year abatement estimated at $500M+; QTS committed not to seek local property tax incentives (per WBRC July 2026) — subsidy excluded as eligibility does not equal award and QTS renounced pursuit. Full buildout timeline: 7+ years. Birmingham Business Alliance estimated ~$4B net benefit over 30 years; Bessemer City Schools projected ~$25M/year. AI framing not officially confirmed by QTS or the city as of July 2026 — size and scale (hyperscale, cloud/AI demand framing in QTS statement) support 'likely' classification. Coordinates are approximate for the Red Ridge parcel area. Jobs: ~1,000 construction and ~330 permanent are projected figures for the proposed (not-yet-built) campus (WBRC, Nov 2025) — recorded as projections in notes, not structured jobs fields, per data-rigor policy.

energy.notes: Alabama Power confirmed 1,200 MW power availability for the campus. QTS committed project-related energy costs will not be passed on to local utility ratepayers. Opponents (NAACP, Black Warrior Riverkeeper, SELC) raised concerns that the facility will rely on fossil fuels. Bibb County residents identified as potentially facing higher power bills due to grid strain.

water.notes: QTS committed to a closed-loop cooling system that does not consume water for cooling once operational. Opposition groups including Black Warrior Riverkeeper raised concerns about water impacts during construction and operation, citing proximity to a Black Warrior River tributary. Tuscaloosa County flagged for potential irreversible water impacts.

community.notes: Strong and ongoing opposition. Public Facebook group 'Bessemer Data Center - We Say No!' grew to 720+ members. NAACP cited environmental justice concerns for majority-minority Bessemer community. Black Warrior Riverkeeper (BWR) and Southern Environmental Law Center (SELC) opposed construction over fossil fuel and water impacts. U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service raised concerns. Rezoning lawsuit filed in Jefferson County Circuit Court (temporarily enjoined; later dismissed). Bessemer City Council approved initial rezoning 5-2 in November 2025 despite crowd opposition (Councilman King and Councilwoman Thigpen dissented). 914-acre expansion rezoning approved April 2026. Some elected officials operated under NDAs with the developer prior to QTS confirmation. Homeowners filed additional lawsuits against the project.

name: Justified Data Campus

statusHistory.note: TeraWulf acquired ~750-acre former Century Aluminum smelter site from Century Aluminum subsidiary; Justified Data campus announced February 2, 2026.

20-year lease signed with Anthropic PBC (via TeraWulf's Raylan Data LLC entity) for 401 MW critical IT load; ~$19B contracted lease revenue over initial term. Initial capacity targeted H2 2027; full 401 MW ramp by early 2028. Announced July 6, 2026.

Big Rivers Electric Corporation — PSC Retail Service Agreement Filing for Justified Data Project
Hawesville smelter to become AI data center; community split on economic impact
TeraWulf signs 20-year Anthropic lease for 401 MW at Kentucky data campus
Anthropic signs $19bn, 20-year lease for Kentucky data center with TeraWulf
AI company Anthropic commits to long-term Hawesville campus

notes: Brownfield redevelopment of former Century Aluminum smelter. Site acquired February 2026 from Century Aluminum subsidiary; acquisition price reported by multiple outlets as $200M cash plus a 6.8% minority equity interest in TeraWulf (not confirmed in official PR text fetched). Lease entity: TeraWulf's Raylan Data LLC to Anthropic PBC. The ~$19B is contracted lease revenue over the 20-year term — not capex; excluded from investmentUsd. TeraWulf indicated a $3B–$4B construction investment for two data center buildings (range, not a firm single figure; excluded from investmentUsd). Site has ~480 MW of existing power availability; 401 MW is the Anthropic lease capacity. Total site ~750 acres per news sources; TeraWulf's official PR cited 'over 250 buildable acres.' Coordinates are approximate (Hawesville town center). Fluidstack/Abernathy JV is a separate unrelated transaction — excluded from this record.

energy.notes: Site connects directly to regional transmission network via on-site energized substation and multiple high-voltage transmission lines, bypassing local distribution. Big Rivers Electric filed a retail service agreement with the Kentucky PSC in April 2026 covering 482 MW transmission capacity for the Justified Data project; TeraWulf bears identified costs and risks for obtaining and transmitting capacity. TeraWulf is covering the full cost of required grid upgrades.

water.notes: TeraWulf stated at community meetings that no river water or municipal water will be used. Advanced closed-loop liquid cooling system; company stated campus will use less water over several years than the former aluminum smelter consumed in a single day. No reported MGD figure available.

community.notes: Online petition with 1,200+ signatures asking county officials, the governor, and the state utility regulator to pause approvals pending disclosure of energy, environmental, and infrastructure impacts. Hancock County Judge-Executive Johnny Roberts publicly supportive, citing 100–120 high-paying permanent jobs and significant new annual tax revenue for schools and emergency services. Opposition concerns center on water use, traffic, and environmental effects. TeraWulf held community meetings (March 2026) to address water and cooling questions.

name: Aligned Data Centers – Conesville Industrial Park

statusHistory.note: Aligned press release; 197-acre campus at Conesville Industrial Park, foundational customer secured, initial delivery mid-2026
Groundbreaking ceremony held at Conesville Industrial Park

sources.label: Aligned Data Centers official press release – Ohio mega-scale AI campus expansion
Aligned to develop 200-acre data center campus in Coshocton, Ohio
Aligned Data Centers expands with new mega-scale AI campus at Conesville Industrial Park
Ground broken for new Aligned Data Centers campus in Conesville
From Brownfield to Breakthrough – Aligned Data Centers extends AI-first vision in Ohio
Coshocton Port Authority secures $58.7M from Ohio Department of Development – All Ohio Future Fund
Aligned Data Centers expands in Ohio, considering nuclear power for resilience

notes: Brownfield redevelopment of former AEP Conesville coal plant (2 GW, operational 62 years, closed May 2020). Frontier Group of Companies acquired the plant in 2020 and built the Conesville Industrial Park across ~2,500 acres; Aligned purchased a 197-acre parcel. Aligned describes the project as 'AI/HPC' and 'Gigascale' in its press materials, and this is its third Ohio campus (others: Perkins Township/Sandusky, 96 MW total; Pataskala). Multi-building campus — renderings show up to four buildings; second, third, and fourth buildings referenced at groundbreaking. Capacity MW not publicly disclosed. Investment described as 'multi-billion dollar' — no firm figure confirmed; field omitted per policy. Construction jobs described as 'thousands'; permanent roles as 'hundreds of high-quality long-term positions' — projections only, numeric fields omitted. Aligned is exploring on-site nuclear power (SMRs) for this campus and nearby Zanesville/Cambridge sites; no agreement confirmed. Coordinates approximate (village-level). Site infrastructure funding note: the Coshocton Port Authority received a $58.7M grant (Ohio Dept of Development All Ohio Future Fund, announced July 21, 2025) for utility upgrades, water/wastewater extensions, and roadway improvements across the broader industrial park — this benefits Aligned indirectly but is not a direct subsidy to Aligned and is therefore not listed under subsidies.

energy.notes: Site is in AEP Ohio service territory. Brownfield location retains former plant's high-capacity grid infrastructure, which is a primary site-selection advantage. Aligned is publicly exploring on-site SMR/nuclear additions but no confirmed agreement. Nuclear would be future supplemental supply.

water.notes: No cooling type or water consumption data disclosed. Former coal plant had access to Muskingum River cooling water infrastructure; Aligned has not confirmed whether this will be utilized.

community.notes: Strong positive reception at October 2025 groundbreaking. Coshocton County Commissioners and Port Authority executive director spoke favorably. Site revitalization fills a significant local tax and employment gap left by AEP plant closure (plant formerly provided ~10% of River View Local Schools' annual budget and employed ~600 workers). Broader Ohio policy context: a proposed constitutional amendment that would ban large data centers was under debate at the time of announcement; some community and policy advocates cited this project as evidence of the economic value data centers bring to rural counties.

name: AVAIO Digital Taurus

statusHistory.note: AVAIO Digital announced $6B AI-ready data center campus in Brandon, Rankin County, MS; Mississippi Development Authority approved Data Center Enterprises tax exemption.
Groundbreaking held at East Metropolitan Center Business and Industrial Park in Brandon.

sources.label: AVAIO Digital Taurus announcement press release
AVAIO Digital Taurus official project page
Mississippi Development Authority project announcement
Governor Reeves office announcement — third-largest project in MS history
sources.publisher: Office of the Governor of Mississippi

sources.label: City of Brandon welcome announcement
Brandon residents petition — water, power, pollution concerns

notes: Distinct from AVAIO's 'Leo' campus in Little Rock/Pulaski County AR — do not conflate. Site: East Metropolitan Center Business and Industrial Park, Brandon. Land acreage: AVAIO's own project page and groundbreaking materials cite 329 acres; the August 19 press release cited 175 acres — 329 used as more authoritative per operator's site. Capacity: 116 MW initial via executed ESA with Entergy Mississippi (energized June 2027 per operator site); Entergy guiding an additional 420 MW of grid power available by end of 2030; access to Gulf South Pipeline for up to 500 MW of on-site behind-the-meter thermal generation starting Q2 2027 — total baseload path to over 1 GW. The 116 MW planned figure reflects the executed first-phase ESA only; full buildout remains a projection. Investment: $6B stated without 'up to' qualifier across operator, MDA, and Governor's office sources; Governor's office describes it as the third-largest economic development project in Mississippi history. Jobs: 'at least 60 direct, high-tech jobs' and 'hundreds of indirect jobs ranging from construction to other related industries' — projections, not firm figures; no construction vs permanent breakdown confirmed. Rooftop solar of 20+ MW planned (behind-the-meter). Recycled water and new sewer line extension cited by operator as water infrastructure options. Estimated $23M in ad valorem tax revenue annually per Rankin First (county economic development group). Coordinates approximate (Brandon city center, Rankin County).

energy.notes: Initial 116 MW grid power via executed ESA with Entergy Mississippi (June 2027 target energization). Additional 420 MW grid power from Entergy projected by end of 2030. On-site thermal generation via Gulf South Pipeline access (up to 500 MW planned, Q2 2027 start) — on-site gas not yet built. Rooftop solar 20+ MW planned. Behind-the-meter generation noted by operator.

water.notes: Operator describes water-efficient cooling, rainwater recapture, cooling system economization (air-side economization implied), and recycled water as an available option. New sewer line extension under construction for wastewater. No specific MGD figure disclosed.

subsidies.program: Mississippi Data Center Enterprises tax exemption (approved by MDA; sales & use tax incentives for campus tenants; multi-year, no confirmed total amount)

community.notes: Strong official support: Governor Reeves, Mayor Butch Lee (Brandon), Rankin County, and MDA all endorsed project. Community opposition emerged before groundbreaking: online petition to Rankin County Board of Supervisors demanded written guarantees against utility bill increases and air/water pollution, and called for construction halt if guarantees cannot be made. Residents cited concerns about water consumption, air pollution (referencing xAI Memphis gas turbine precedent), power demands, and traffic. Community group 'Mississippi For Mississippi' produced an impact-distance map. Rankin County supervisors did not respond to press inquiries. No litigation reported.

name: AWS Project Galaxy — Sidney, OH

statusHistory.note: Sidney City Council approved 30-year 100% property tax abatement and development agreement; NDA executed mid-2025
Construction began January 2026; site clearing and earthworks underway by March 2026; initial operations targeted Sept 15, 2026

sources.label: Amazon secures tax break for $3bn data center campus in Sidney, Ohio
Project Galaxy Amazon Data Center — Shelby County
Amazon: Project Galaxy Data Center
FERC urged to shield AES Ohio customers from grid costs for 1.5-GW Amazon data center

notes: Codename 'Project Galaxy.' Coordinates approximate (parcel: north of Millcreek Road, west of Vandemark Road, Sidney OH). AES Ohio FERC filing shows phased power ramp: 65 MW at initial operations (Sept 15, 2026 target), 480 MW within one year, 1,500 MW by January 2031 — 1,500 MW taken as planned total capacity. $3B investment described as a firm commitment in the approved development agreement (not 'up to'). AWS has committed an estimated $23B in Ohio data centers through 2029–2030. Baxtel lists a 'West Hub' expansion site associated with this campus. FERC filing lists location as 'Jefferson Township, Ohio'; confirmed as Sidney/Shelby County campus from all other sources.

energy.notes: AES Ohio confirmed as primary grid supplier per FERC filing; Phase 1 network upgrade costs $22.8M recovered via Transmission Cost Recovery Rider (TCRR). AEP Ohio's 85% data-center tariff (eff. July 23, 2025) may apply given service-territory overlap corridor. AEP Ohio onsite Bloom Energy fuel cells approved for some AWS Ohio locations (PUCO order), but not confirmed specifically for this Sidney site.

water.notes: City of Sidney public water system (groundwater wells, Great Miami River, Tawawa Creek). Development agreement caps withdrawal at 1.0 million gallons per day (694 gpm maximum); projected average annual usage is 4.6 million gallons per year (~12,600 gpd average). All wastewater including cooling discharge routed to city sanitary sewer. Miami Valley Conservancy District raised concerns citing peak draw up to 300 gpm. Cooling type inferred from industrial-scale water-in / wastewater-out pattern; not explicitly stated in sources.

subsidies.program: 30-year 100% real-property tax abatement (est. $180M–$350M foregone over term); $50M PILOT payment by AWS to City of Sidney ($25M) and Sidney City Schools ($25M) over 15 years

community.notes: Active opposition from 'Sidney Citizens for Responsible Development' (founded by Kristin Allen, 4th-generation farm family) and 'Sidney Citizens Data Center Watch' (150+ Facebook members). Core concerns: per-job tax abatement economics ($2.4M–$4.6M in foregone tax revenue per permanent job), water capacity strain, and NDA limiting public transparency during approval process. Community town hall held March 6, 2026 with Shelby County Commissioners attending; no Sidney City Council members attended. City published FAQ in response to community pressure.

Facility address: 512 NW 62nd St (corner of NW 61st/62nd St and N Lee Ave), Oklahoma City. Ribbon cutting September 22, 2025. Houses 300+ CS-3 Wafer Scale Engine systems delivering 44+ exaflops of AI compute. Facility features Tier III-quality infrastructure, tornado and seismic shielding, triple-redundant power stations. Cerebras matches all kWh consumption with renewable energy (REC-based, not on-site generation). OG&E reported as grid utility — consistent with OKC service territory but not explicitly named in Cerebras primary sources. Investment amount not disclosed. Jobs: Cerebras blog references 'hundreds of good-paying jobs' in engineering, IT, and operations plus local college workforce partnerships — no firm headcount confirmed. Coordinates are approximate (dispatch seed); actual facility is at 512 NW 62nd St.

energy.notes: Every kWh matched with renewable energy (REC-based offset; no on-site generation disclosed). OG&E (Oklahoma Gas and Electric) reported as serving utility — consistent with OKC service territory; not explicitly named in Cerebras primary sources but cited in secondary coverage.

water.notes: Direct-to-chip liquid cooling via closed-loop system; Cerebras describes it as minimizing water consumption. Custom water-cooling solution designed for CS-3 wafer-scale servers. No mgd figure disclosed.

community.notes: Cerebras reports workforce development partnerships with local colleges and technical programs; 'hundreds of good-paying jobs' in engineering, IT, and operations noted on official blog. No public opposition or contention reported in any source reviewed.

Warrenton Planning & Zoning Commission approved conditional use permit and site plan 8-2 on January 8, 2026; Board of Aldermen approved unanimously (two 6-0 votes) on January 20, 2026. Still requires land disturbance review and construction permits before groundbreaking.

notes: Coordinates approximate. Site address: 22151 NW Service Road, Warrenton MO; north of NW Service Road, west of Hwy. W. Two 805,000 sq ft data center buildings + 40,000 sq ft administration building + 621,250 sq ft Ameren-owned switch yard + 603,200 sq ft Crusoe-owned electrical substation. Crusoe described as its 'fifth campus' in its national AI data center portfolio as of June 2026 per Crusoe newsroom. Meta signed agreement with Crusoe in June 2026 for approximately 1.6GW of AI computing capacity split across Childress TX and Warrenton MO campuses — per-site split not disclosed; confirms Warrenton as AI anchor customer site (sourceRef 3). Status: conditional use permit and site plan approved; land disturbance review and construction permits still outstanding before groundbreaking. Crusoe committed publicly to seek no real property tax or gross receipt tax abatements; would pursue state incentives only (sourceRef 2). Developer projected annual property taxes ~$80M/year (sourceRef 1), with ~$44M to local schools. Construction jobs projected ~2,000-2,500 over ~3-year build; permanent tech jobs projected ~100 (developer estimates, sourceRef 1 and 2 — not firm contractual commitments). Crusoe historically used on-site flared natural gas but divested those operations; Warrenton campus is grid-connected via Ameren Missouri — no on-site gas generation reported here. Distinct from the Google/Crusoe 'Goodnight' campus in Armstrong County TX.

energy.notes: Ameren Missouri owns and will operate the on-site switch yard; Crusoe owns the on-site electrical substation. Ameren signed confidential large-load agreements totaling 2.2GW with multiple Missouri data centers in February 2026; individual customer details not disclosed (sourceRef 8). No on-site gas generation reported for this campus.

water.notes: Closed-loop cooling system using food-grade polypropylene glycol continuously recycled within the system; no reported MGD figure disclosed. Company claims 40% less water usage compared to a conventional light industrial facility (sourceRef 2).

community.notes: Over 40 community members testified during the January 20, 2026 Board of Aldermen hearing — both supporters and opponents. Supporters cited economic development and tax revenue; opponents (including neighboring landowners) raised concerns about noise, light pollution, and water supply impacts. Planning & Zoning voted 8-2 to approve; Board of Aldermen approved unanimously (6-0) with conditions: additional native trees, shorter lighting poles, stricter noise limits, generators moved indoors. One opponent stated: 'The county residents surrounding this parcel have been ignored and forgotten' (sourceRef 2).

notes: Investment described as '$1 billion' in RFCDA announcement; Data Center Dynamics and Business Facilities use 'more than $1 billion' framing — treated as a floor, not a firm single figure (investmentUsd omitted per data policy). Completion targeted 2027–2028. Site is 347 acres on Huffaker Road, east of Fouche Gap Road, in unincorporated Floyd County, adjacent to Georgia Power-owned land; land purchased at $40,000/acre (~$13.9M). Microsoft stated site selection was driven by 'Rome and Floyd County's industrial zoning and strong infrastructure.' Microsoft committed to covering all costs for additional infrastructure (power, water, roads) so ratepayers and taxpayers are not burdened. Part of Microsoft's broader East US 3 Georgia cloud build-out; announced to bolster Azure cloud services. Floyd County enacted an 80-day moratorium on new data center permits (2025) but explicitly noted previously filed projects including Microsoft's can proceed. Capacity MW not disclosed. Coordinates are approximate; actual parcel is on Huffaker Road in unincorporated Floyd County.

energy.notes: No public utility service agreement confirmed as of 2026-07-07. Georgia Power is the regional electric utility for Floyd County. A January 2025 Georgia PSC rule approved special terms for new large data center customers using >100 MW.

water.notes: No water usage figures or cooling system type disclosed publicly as of 2026-07-07.

subsidies.program: 12-year partial property tax abatement on buildings + 9-year partial abatement on equipment, granted by Floyd County Development Authority via industrial revenue bonds; land value taxes and Floyd County school taxes remain payable on the land portion

community.notes: RFCDA chair Jimmy Byars: 'This is a tremendous economic opportunity for our community that will have positive impacts for decades to come.' Floyd County Schools and Rome City Schools superintendents both expressed enthusiasm for workforce training partnership. Microsoft Director of Community Engagement Paul Englis noted 150 jobs is likely 'a bit on the low side.' No opposition documented at October 2023 announcement. Floyd County's 2025 moratorium on new data centers was not directed at the Microsoft project, which had already been filed.

name: La Osa Data Center Park
poweredBy: WAPA grid interconnect; on-site natural gas (phase 1); solar (planned)

statusHistory.note: Vermaland publicly announces La Osa Project (DCD: Aug 4, 2025; AZ Big Media: Sep 15, 2025); rezoning application filed with Pinal County ~April 2025. $33B/3 GW ceiling figures publicized. Pinal County Planning & Zoning Commission holds non-voting informational session July 17, 2025.

April 17, 2026: Pinal County land-use committee recommends advancing to full rezoning and site-plan review. No construction authorization granted; comprehensive plan amendment, drainage, flood mitigation, and road improvements still required.

May 2026: Operator announces 80% scale-back after all ~60 public comments opposed the project — revised plan reduces to 11 data centers and a 1 GW energy demand ceiling (down from 59 DCs / 3 GW). Board of Supervisors hearing rescheduled to August 26, 2026.

sources.label: Land developer Vermaland pitches $33bn data center in Arizona
$33 billion data center industrial park coming to Pinal County
Largest proposed data center in Arizona to be scaled back 80% after opposition
Pinal County Advances Vermaland's La Osa Data Center Plan
$33B Data Center Park Eyed in Arizona

notes: Vermaland LLC is a Phoenix-area land developer and land-banking company (CEO Kuldip 'Ken' Verma, founded 2006, controls ~25,000 acres of metro Phoenix land). DCD explicitly noted 'it is unclear whether Vermaland would operate its own data center facilities' — Vermaland plans to sell or rent parcels/buildings to tenants. No end tenants or hyperscaler operators have been named as of July 2026. Original plan: 59 data centers, up to 3 GW capacity ceiling, up to $33B investment ceiling, 3,300-acre site south of Eloy along the Greene Canal. A separate adjacent 1,700-acre parcel already holds Industrial-2 zoning for data centers, gas plants, battery storage, and solar. After strong public opposition (all ~60 public comments opposed), operator announced May 2026 scale-back to 11 data centers and 1 GW ceiling. Board of Supervisors hearing scheduled August 26, 2026; no permits issued as of record date. Site lies adjacent to a Qualified Opportunity Zone (investor tax benefit; not a direct subsidy to Vermaland — no subsidy award confirmed, omitted). Coordinates are approximate (parcel south of Eloy city center along Greene Canal; no precise APN published). $33B investment and all GW figures are announced ceilings, not firm commitments.

energy.utility: Western Area Power Administration (WAPA) — proposed grid transmission line upgrades for interconnect. APS and SRP not confirmed as utility providers.
energy.notes: Phase 1 would rely on on-site natural gas turbines as primary power source, transitioning to solar as adjacent solar projects come online. Utility-scale battery storage included in original 3 GW plan. Hybrid model: gas + solar + battery + WAPA grid. On-site generation MW not specified in any source.

water.notes: Developer's attorney stated commitment to air-cooled or closed-loop cooling to minimize water demand. Site borders the Greene Canal, raising concerns about water access and consumption in an arid region. No MGD figures have been published. Critics cited Pima County's 'Project Blue' hyperscale data center as a cautionary precedent for water misuse in Arizona desert developments.

community.notes: All approximately 60 public comments and 50 emails received by the Pinal County Board opposed the project. Concerns raised: location in a high-risk flood zone, wildlife and environmental impacts, water use risk, power bill impacts for local residents, construction timeline (gas plant likely last, 5-7 year timeline). Scale-back from 3 GW to 1 GW was a direct response to this opposition. Pinal County Supervisor Rich Vitiello expressed concern about constituent energy cost impacts.

notes: Operational since 2013; 5M+ sq ft after seven expansions — Meta's largest cloud campus. Meta's engineering blog documents Altoona as a GenAI infrastructure / LLaMA training site. INVESTMENT: 'more than $2.5 billion' is a cumulative multi-year figure across all expansions (in notes, not investmentUsd). 400+ on-site operations staff is a cumulative daily figure (in notes). ~350 MW per third-party index. water.notes: Evaporative cooling with outside-air economization ~93% of the year (Meta design). Altoona utility director Daniel Scott: the campus 'can use up to a million gallons of water a day, one fifth of what the city can produce' - a permit peak/ceiling, not a measured average; a city agreement caps Meta at up to 16% of Altoona's supply. Water drawn from the Jordan Aquifer. No verified operational daily-use figure has been disclosed.

subsidies.program: City of Altoona 20-year property-tax waiver (Building 5 / Siculus LLC, ~1M sq ft) - Meta instead pays ~$3/sq ft (~$3M/yr), roughly a 40% discount; IEDA High Quality Jobs program
Iowa data-center sales & use tax exemption (Iowa Code sec. 423.3(95)) on equipment, cooling, and electricity - eligibility met; per-campus dollar value undisclosed

jobs.notes: Meta: 'more than 400 people work on-site in data center operations each day' (operational count). Construction peaked ~1,300 trades and averaged ~1,000 across 12+ years of continuous building - ranges -> notes.

community.notes: Altoona Mayor Dean O'Connor, initially opposed, became supportive as the campus grew; Meta donated two water towers to the city and has given $4M+ locally; LEED Gold buildings. Resident-level concerns (water share, electricity rates, noise/light) are documented but no organized opposition or litigation was found.

notes: 615-acre, up-to-13-building QTS-built campus on Hwy 54W near Fayetteville, Fayette County (Microsoft's materials say 'Atlanta'). Began operation Oct 2025 as Fairwater 2 — a 'multi-gigawatt' AI campus. ~1,500 MW is the anticipated ultimate Georgia Power load across all buildings (planned figure; ~433 MW cited as initially online). Distinct from Microsoft's smaller Tyrone, GA campus. Two-story design with no UPS or backup generators. Coordinates are the Fayetteville area centroid. Microsoft has not disclosed tenant capex for the Fairwater Fayetteville space; the ~$1B/$6.5B figures are QTS developer build-out estimates for the full multi-tenant campus, and the widely-cited $14.5M abatement / $1.8B / $75M figures attach to different Microsoft/Fulton-County sites (the $75M was FCDA land-sale proceeds, not a subsidy to QTS) -> investmentUsd omitted.

energy.notes: Two-story design with no UPS or backup generators (DCD, Oct 2025) — no on-site generation. Microsoft states Fairwater uses improved closed-loop cooling that 'consumes almost zero water in its operation.' Georgia Power 2025 IRP testimony cites the QTS Fayetteville campus as a single load of just under 1,500 MW at full build-out.

water.notes: QTS closed-loop 'water-free' design (COO Ryan Hunter: 'Once the water's in there, it stays in there for the life of the asset'); ~600,000-gal one-time fill per building, ongoing per-building use 'equivalent to four American households per month.' During construction the campus drew ~29-30M gallons through two unmetered connections (2024-25); Fayette County issued a ~$147,000 retroactive charge and no fine. No operational daily figure for the Microsoft tenant space specifically.

subsidies.program: Fayette County Development Authority 10-year stepped property-tax abatement (0% yr 1 -> 100% yr 10, +10%/yr) on the QTS 'Project Excalibur' campus; also qualifies for Georgia's statewide data-center sales-and-use tax exemption

jobs.notes: QTS (the developer) job figures for the full 13-16-building campus have shifted repeatedly - 100 (2022 city estimate) -> 200 (June 2025 FCDA) -> '800 to 1,000' (QTS written statement to WBRC, Feb 2026) - with no written commitment and none confirmed for the Microsoft Fairwater tenant space; peak construction workforce estimated ~8,000 daily across the full campus. All projections -> honest-zero on the numeric fields.

community.notes: Fayetteville adopted a 90-day moratorium (Jan 29 2026) then unanimously passed Ordinance 26-O-12 (March 5 2026) banning new data centers in all zoning districts; existing Fairwater 1/2 and the Dec 2025 East Campus rezoning are grandfathered. Opposition centered on construction traffic, the ~29M-gal unmetered water draw, and new 500 kV transmission lines; Mayor Edward Johnson expressed support. A competing Crow Holdings proposal withdrew its appeal after the ban. No litigation against the Fairwater campus.

notes: PHX-13, a 72 MW campus in Glendale, Maricopa County. Coordinates are the Glendale area centroid. Land acquired for $86.5M (100 acres at 8785 N Reems Rd, Dec 2024, from LXP Industrial Trust) - land cost, not campus capex -> investmentUsd omitted. No job counts disclosed -> jobs honest-zero. The campus likely uses Arizona's statutory data-center TPT exemption (A.R.S. 41-1519), but no ACA certification or GPLET agreement for this campus is public -> subsidy honest-zero.

energy.notes: APS is building a new 230 kV transmission line to serve the campus (DCNN); grid-sourced renewables, no on-site generation.

water.notes: Aligned Delta-cube closed-loop air-cooling arrays with water recycling (no evaporative draw); DeltaFlow liquid cooling planned for dense AI workloads. A marketed '>73% water reduction' is versus the site's prior agricultural (rose-farm) use, not a data-center withdrawal figure -> no MGD recorded.

community.notes: Glendale Mayor Jerry Weiers attended and praised the April 2025 groundbreaking; the City of Glendale issued its own release. No opposition, contested vote, or litigation documented.

name: Riverview Innovation & Technology Campus
poweredBy: Grid (National Grid); Niagara Power Project + Nine Mile Point Nuclear Station upstream mix

statusHistory.note: Hundreds of residents packed a Town of Tonawanda Planning Board meeting on June 3, 2026 opposing the proposed 300MW data center at the former Tonawanda Coke site; the Planning Board voted to table further discussion pending a revised site plan application.

Developer says the project is 'not on hold' but SEQRA/site plan review is temporarily paused while awaiting NYISO and National Grid clarification on transmission-upgrade costs and timeline; Town Supervisor John Flynn said NYISO's review could take 'several months.'

sources.label: Developer: Proposed $2B AI data center on River Road not on hold, process temporarily delayed
River Road data center on hold amid outcry from residents scarred by Tonawanda Coke fight
Data center project at former Tonawanda Coke site 'on hold'
Residents pack meeting to oppose proposed data center at Tonawanda Coke site
Former coke plant near Buffalo eyed for 300MW data center

energy.notes: Developer states ~91% emission-free electricity due to proximity to the Niagara Power Project (hydro) and Nine Mile Point Nuclear Station. SEQRA/site plan review temporarily paused while awaiting NYISO and National Grid clarification on transmission-upgrade costs/timeline. A $50M on-site electrical substation is planned.

water.notes: Community opposition specifically cited cooling-system noise (24/7 operation, cited by residents as 75-105+ decibels) as a top concern at the June 3, 2026 Planning Board meeting; specific cooling technology not yet disclosed by the developer.

community.notes: Hundreds of residents packed a June 3, 2026 Town of Tonawanda Planning Board meeting to oppose the project; concerns included cooling-system noise, comparisons to the Love Canal environmental disaster, and grid-strain concerns raised by Clean Air Coalition board chair Jim Jones. NY State Senator Jeremy Zellner (co-sponsor of a first-in-the-nation statewide data center moratorium bill) and Assemblyman Bill Conrad have both raised concerns publicly. Planning Board tabled further discussion pending a revised site plan application.

notes: Site: former Tonawanda Coke Corporation coke-manufacturing plant (operated 1917-2018; TCC bankruptcy), 3875 & 3800 River Road, Town of Tonawanda, Erie County NY - 140 acres. Brownfield developer Jon M. Williams completed acquisition Oct. 10, 2019 and has led an estimated $100M remediation; Honeywell (successor to Allied Chemical/TCC predecessor) also involved. 500,000 sq ft facility proposed. Coordinates are approximate for the River Road parcel. Note: one source names 'Niagara County' but Buffalo News, BTPM, and WBEN independently and consistently identify this as the Town of Tonawanda, Erie County (distinct from the separate City of North Tonawanda in Niagara County) - Erie County used here per majority/independent-source cross-check.

name: Luckett Road Data Centers (Fremont Peak Properties)

statusHistory.note: Marana Town Council voted 6-0 to rezone 661 acres along Luckett Road for the data center campus (Fremont Peak Properties/Beale Infrastructure), following applications submitted in October 2025 and packed public testimony on water, power, and growth concerns.

Opposition group Worker Power submitted referendum petitions challenging the rezoning; Marana officials rejected the referendum on the grounds that petitions lacked required legal property descriptions, ending the challenge.

sources.label: Rezoning approved for Marana data center campus

energy.utility: Trico Electric Cooperative (north parcel); Tucson Electric Power (south parcel)
energy.notes: Trico Electric Cooperative to provide 550-750MW to the north parcel; Tucson Electric Power to provide a 'matching amount' to the south parcel (combined range roughly 1,100-1,500MW; no single confirmed total given, so omitted from capacityMw).

water.notes: Marana town code prohibits use of potable water for cooling systems; project proposed as air-cooled. Additional water for irrigation/industrial/fire suppression to be provided by the Cortaro Marana Irrigation District.

community.notes: Marana Town Council approved rezoning 6-0 after hours of packed public testimony on water, power, and growth concerns. Opposition group Worker Power organized a referendum petition challenge; Marana officials rejected the referendum on a technicality (petition lacked required legal property descriptions), ending the challenge. Marana resident and cybersecurity expert Jackie McGuire raised concerns specifically about Blue Owl Capital (Beale Infrastructure's parent).

notes: Two parcels along Luckett Road: north parcel owned by the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (borders Pinal County); south parcel owned by the Kai Family Trust. Fremont Peak Properties LLC (also referenced as 'Humphrey's Peak Properties' in some coverage) is a subsidiary of Beale Infrastructure, backed by Blue Owl Capital; connected to, but a distinct site from, the separately-tracked 'Project Blue' Beale/Blue Owl effort in unincorporated Pima County near Tucson, and from Beale's separately-tracked Project Pilot in De Soto, KS. Developer projects ~400 permanent and ~4,200 temporary construction jobs (Chamber of Southern Arizona estimate) - not yet delivered, so omitted from the jobs field. No explicit AI/GPU branding found in coverage; classified 'likely' given Blue Owl Capital's profile as a major AI-datacenter-era infrastructure investor. Coordinates are approximate for the Luckett Road parcels.

name: Project Hazelnut

statusHistory.note: Hazle Township denied NorthPoint's land development application for the 15-building, ~1,283-acre campus; NorthPoint appealed in Luzerne County Court of Common Pleas and created a $30M fund to offset potential resident utility-rate impacts during the appeal.
Hazle Township passed a 6-month moratorium/curative amendment pausing data center development.

sources.label: Northpoint proposes 15-building, 1,280-acre data center campus in Pennsylvania
Hazle Township, PA Pauses Data Center Development for Six Months
Hazle Township data center developer announces community development package
Project Hazelnut (Hazle Twp - Denied, On Appeal)
Project Hazelnut - official project site

community.notes: Hazle Township denied NorthPoint's land development application; NorthPoint is appealing in Luzerne County Court of Common Pleas and created a $30M fund to offset potential short-term utility rate increases for residents during the appeal. Separately, NorthPoint committed to a $165M Community Benefit Fund: $45M Direct Resident Grant Fund ($10,000 grants to eligible households), $15M Community Support Fund (education/workforce training/arts/recreation/youth), and a $105M long-term commitment ($7M/year for 15 years) toward a new local police department and reduced trash fees. A judge reportedly rebuked NorthPoint's appeal effort. Hazle Township subsequently passed a 6-month moratorium/curative amendment; PA state Sen. Katie Muth has filed a statewide 3-year data center moratorium bill.

notes: Site: Humboldt North Industrial Park, Hazle Township, Luzerne County PA (within a larger ~3,000-acre industrial park); a few miles from Amazon's nuclear-adjacent data center campus (acquired from Talen Energy) - proximity only, AWS is NOT a confirmed tenant/operator for Project Hazelnut; NorthPoint's own project site names no end-tenant, describing this as a speculative build. Phased: Phase 1 (5 buildings) to begin Q4 2026, Phase 2 (3 buildings) Q1 2028, Phase 3 (7 buildings) Q2 2029. Developer projects ~900-1,300 construction jobs and ~900-950 permanent jobs ($50K-$158K salary range) - not yet delivered, so omitted from the jobs field; similarly $13.47M in projected county tax revenue over 20 years is a projection, not a subsidy. First surfaced publicly in connection with PA's Permit Fast Track Program (Executive Order 2024-04). NorthPoint's own site explicitly frames this as a general 'data center campus' supporting 'cloud platforms, digital payments, streaming, and healthcare systems' - no AI-specific language found; classified 'mixed_use' given hyperscale-tier campus scale makes AI workloads plausible even absent explicit branding.
